Costs scale with parameters × tokens — Llama 3 405B used roughly 4 × 10^25 FLOPs, or tens of millions of dollars of H100 time. After pretraining you have a base model that will complete text but not follow instructions.
why_it_matters: Pretraining is where capability comes from. Fine-tuning polishes; pretraining establishes. You cannot compensate for a weak base model with clever post-training.
common_misconceptions: A pretrained base model is usually useless as a chatbot — it will complete your prompt literally, not answer it. Instruct-tuning is a separate step.

title: Post-Training
short_definition: Everything done to a base model after pretraining — SFT, DPO/RLHF, safety tuning — to turn it into a useful assistant.
full_explanation: Post-training is the alignment and capability-refinement phase. Typical pipeline: SFT on high-quality instruction-response pairs → preference optimization (DPO, RLHF, or newer variants like ORPO) → safety tuning (constitutional AI, red-teaming loops) → specialized capabilities like tool use or long-context. Costs are a fraction of pretraining (millions vs. tens of millions), but this stage determines everything a user touches: helpfulness, harmlessness, style, refusal behavior.
why_it_matters: Two labs with the same base model but different post-training produce entirely different products. It is where the personality and safety of an assistant live.
common_misconceptions: Post-training cannot teach new facts — if the base model never saw something, post-training often just teaches it to confidently make it up.

title: Instruction Tuning
short_definition: Fine-tuning a base LLM on (instruction, response) pairs so it reliably follows user requests instead of just completing text.
full_explanation: Instruction tuning — basically SFT with instruction-shaped data — converts a base model into an assistant. The canonical recipe: 10k–1M high-quality examples, formatted with the model's chat template (system/user/assistant turns), trained for a few epochs at low learning rate. Datasets range from human-written (Dolly, OpenAssistant) to synthetically generated (Self-Instruct, Evol-Instruct, UltraChat) to distilled from stronger models (Alpaca, Vicuna). Quality matters more than quantity past roughly 10k well-curated examples.
why_it_matters: Without instruction tuning, a base LLM is close to unusable for chat. This step delivers most of the user-visible 'it feels smart' improvement.
common_misconceptions: More instruction data is not strictly better. Low-quality synthetic data can teach the model a bland, sycophantic style.

title: Reasoning (LLM Reasoning)
short_definition: A model's ability to solve multi-step problems — math, logic, planning — by thinking through intermediate steps rather than pattern-matching to an answer.
full_explanation: Reasoning in LLMs emerged with chain-of-thought prompting and matured with models like o1, DeepSeek-R1, and Claude that are trained explicitly to produce long internal reasoning traces before answering. Techniques: chain-of-thought (write out steps), self-consistency (sample multiple paths, vote), tree-of-thought (explore branches), reflection (critique and revise). Reinforcement learning on correctness signals (process reward models, outcome reward models) is the current frontier for pushing reasoning further.
why_it_matters: Reasoning is the difference between a model that solves a trivia question and one that can debug code, prove theorems, or plan a multi-step agent task.
common_misconceptions: LLM 'reasoning' is still token prediction — it can look convincing and be wrong. Long chains of thought do not imply correctness.

title: Tree of Thought
short_definition: A reasoning strategy where the model explores a tree of intermediate thoughts, evaluating and backtracking, rather than following a single linear chain.
full_explanation: Tree-of-thought (ToT, Yao et al. 2023) generalizes chain-of-thought into a search problem. At each step, the model proposes multiple candidate next thoughts, scores them (self-evaluation or heuristic), and expands the most promising ones — classic BFS or DFS over a thought tree. ToT improves solve rates on tasks that need planning or backtracking (Game of 24, creative writing) at the cost of many more LLM calls per problem. Often paired with value functions or learned critics to prune.
why_it_matters: For hard planning tasks, a single chain of thought often goes down a wrong path and never recovers. ToT adds the ability to backtrack.
common_misconceptions: ToT is not free — it can multiply inference cost 10–100x and is overkill for simple questions. Use when single-shot CoT plateaus.

title: Self-Consistency
short_definition: A reasoning trick where the model samples multiple chains of thought at nonzero temperature and picks the answer that appears most often.
full_explanation: Self-consistency (Wang et al. 2022) exploits the observation that a correct answer can be reached via many different reasoning paths, while wrong answers often scatter across unrelated reasoning. Sample N chains of thought with temperature 0.7, extract the final answer from each, take a majority vote. Gains on GSM8K, MATH, and multi-hop QA are consistent and often exceed 10 percentage points over greedy CoT. Cost is linear in N — typically N between 5 and 40.
why_it_matters: It is the cheapest single knob that turns a weaker reasoning model into a much better one. Costs only extra tokens, not training.
common_misconceptions: Self-consistency needs majority-voteable answers — it works for numeric or classification outputs, poorly for free-form essays.
code_example:
answers = []
for _ in range(10):
    resp = llm.complete(prompt, temperature=0.7)
    answers.append(extract_answer(resp))
final = Counter(answers).most_common(1)[0][0]

title: ReAct Pattern
short_definition: A prompting pattern that interleaves Reasoning steps with Action steps (tool calls), letting an LLM iteratively gather info before answering.
full_explanation: ReAct (Yao et al. 2022) structures agent steps as Thought → Action → Observation cycles. The model writes a thought about what it needs, emits an action (e.g. 'search[query]'), a tool runs and returns an observation, and the model continues thinking with the new evidence. This grounds reasoning in external facts and is the foundation of most modern agent frameworks — LangChain, LlamaIndex, AutoGen, and LangGraph all owe a debt to ReAct.
why_it_matters: ReAct is the mental model for nearly every LLM-plus-tools system in production. It keeps the LLM as the planner and offloads facts to tools.
common_misconceptions: ReAct is not a training objective — it is a prompting convention. Newer models natively support tool-calling APIs that make the 'parse action from text' part unnecessary.

title: Reflection (Self-Reflection)
short_definition: A technique where an LLM critiques its own output and revises it, often improving quality at the cost of extra inference calls.
full_explanation: Reflection loops run: generate → self-critique → revise. The critique prompt asks the model to identify errors, missing reasoning, or stylistic issues in its own draft; the revision prompt fixes them. Variants like Reflexion add memory so the agent learns from past mistakes within a session. Works best when the critique is grounded in something concrete — test outputs, retrieval hits, rubric scores — not just the model's aesthetic judgment. Self-rewarding language models extend this to training.
why_it_matters: Reflection gives you a second look without a second model. For code, drafts, and plans it can catch 20–40% of errors a single pass misses.
common_misconceptions: A model's self-critique is only as reliable as its evaluation ability. Models bad at a task are also bad at critiquing that task.

title: Prompt Chaining
short_definition: Decomposing a task into a sequence of simpler LLM calls where each output feeds the next.
full_explanation: Prompt chaining turns a big fuzzy prompt into a pipeline of focused ones: extract → classify → summarize → format, or plan → draft → revise. Each step is easier for the model than the monolithic request, and each has a checkable output. Chains can be linear, branching (conditional routing), or DAG-shaped. Tools like LangChain, Prefect AI, and DSPy exist specifically to build and monitor chains.
why_it_matters: Chains let weaker or cheaper models do work that would otherwise need frontier-model single-shot calls. They also make each step testable.
common_misconceptions: Longer chains are not always better — each hop adds latency and compounds error. Use the shortest chain that hits quality.

title: Orchestration (Agent Orchestration)
short_definition: The control layer that decides what step an AI system takes next — which model, which tool, which branch, and when to stop.
full_explanation: Orchestration sits between your application and your LLMs, tools, and memory stores. It handles routing (which model for this request), retries, timeouts, fallback chains, parallelism, streaming back to the UI, and state management between steps. Frameworks like LangGraph, Temporal, Inngest, and Dagster offer durable execution so an agent can survive crashes. For simple apps, direct code works. For multi-step agents, durable orchestration becomes critical — a crash mid-plan without replayable state means restarting the whole task.
why_it_matters: As agent workflows grow past 2–3 steps, informal code breaks down. Orchestration is the difference between a demo and a production agent.
common_misconceptions: Orchestration is not 'just chaining prompts' — it includes state, retries, and durability concerns that typical framework tutorials skip.

title: Multimodal
short_definition: Models or systems that jointly process multiple input modalities — text, image, audio, video, code — in a single forward pass.
full_explanation: A multimodal model encodes each modality into a shared representation space, typically by mapping images through a vision encoder (CLIP-style) whose features are projected into the LLM's token embedding space. GPT-4o, Gemini 2, Claude 3+, and Llama 4 Scout/Maverick are natively multimodal. Capabilities: describe images, read charts, answer visual questions, transcribe speech, analyze video. Training requires paired data (image+caption, audio+transcript) at massive scale.
why_it_matters: The world is not text. Multimodality is the unlock for document understanding, robotics, accessibility, and anything involving screens.
common_misconceptions: Multimodal does not mean 'equally good at all modalities' — most models are weaker on video and on-device audio than on text.

title: Vision Language Model
short_definition: A multimodal model that understands both images and text in a single architecture, typically by projecting image features into the LLM's embedding space.
full_explanation: A VLM combines a vision encoder (often a ViT or SigLIP) with a language model. Images are split into patches, encoded into visual tokens, projected into the LLM's embedding dimension, and prepended to text tokens. The LLM then attends over the combined sequence. Examples: LLaVA (open), GPT-4o, Claude 3+, Gemini, Qwen-VL, Llama 4. Training stages: align vision encoder to LLM (contrastive or projection pretraining), then instruction-tune on image+text tasks.
why_it_matters: VLMs power document understanding, UI agents, accessibility tools, and multimodal search. They have replaced OCR pipelines for most tasks.
common_misconceptions: VLMs still hallucinate about image content — they can miss objects, misread charts, or invent text not in the image. Always verify for high-stakes use.

title: Agent Loop
short_definition: The core execution cycle of an AI agent — observe state, decide on an action, execute a tool, incorporate the result, repeat.
full_explanation: Every agent framework, no matter the marketing, boils down to this loop: (1) present current state to the LLM, (2) LLM chooses an action — usually a tool call or final answer, (3) execute the action if it is a tool, (4) append the result to the state, (5) loop until the agent outputs a terminal action or hits a step/time/cost budget. Stopping conditions matter: without a hard cap, agents can loop forever on failures. Modern patterns wrap this loop in durable execution so it survives restarts.
why_it_matters: Understanding the agent loop lets you debug any agent framework. Every bug — hallucinated tool, wrong arguments, infinite loop — shows up as a step in this cycle.

title: Autonomous Agent
short_definition: An AI system that pursues a goal with minimal human intervention, planning and executing multi-step work across tools and time.
full_explanation: Autonomous agents combine planning, tool use, memory, and self-direction. They are given a goal (not a task) and decompose it themselves. Classic examples include AutoGPT-style agents, research agents like GPT Researcher, and coding agents like Claude Code, Devin, and Cursor. The main failure modes are goal drift (pursuing wrong sub-goals), error accumulation (wrong step poisons later steps), and runaway cost. Production deployments add checkpoints, human approval for dangerous actions, and tight tool-permission scopes.
why_it_matters: Autonomous agents change the economics of knowledge work — tasks that took hours can run unattended. They also introduce new risks that need new guardrails.
common_misconceptions: Autonomy is a spectrum, not a switch. Most production 'agents' are semi-autonomous with human checkpoints, not fully unattended.

title: Copilot Pattern
short_definition: A human-in-the-loop UX where the AI suggests, edits, and assists in real time but the user retains control and approves every action.
full_explanation: The copilot pattern — GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Notion AI, Google Docs Smart Compose — optimizes for augmentation rather than automation. Characteristics: inline suggestions, accept/reject on every change, model runs in the user's editing context, minimal friction to invoke. Contrasts with agent patterns, which assume long-running autonomous execution. Copilots typically succeed in latency-sensitive creative work where taste matters.
why_it_matters: For tasks where judgment is required and errors are expensive, copilots win. Most production AI today is copilot-shaped, not agent-shaped.

title: Human-in-the-Loop
short_definition: A system design where human judgment is required for specific steps — labeling, approval, escalation — rather than full automation.
full_explanation: Human-in-the-loop (HITL) systems combine AI speed with human judgment at the points where errors are most costly. Common patterns: confidence thresholds that route uncertain cases to reviewers, periodic audits of AI decisions, active learning that asks humans to label the examples the model finds most confusing, approval gates before irreversible actions. HITL is the dominant pattern in regulated industries (healthcare, finance, legal) and for any action that writes to the real world.
why_it_matters: Pure automation fails on edge cases with high stakes. HITL is how you ship AI in production without unbounded risk.

title: Modality
short_definition: A single channel of information a model consumes or produces — text, image, audio, video, code, structured data.
full_explanation: Each modality has different statistical structure and requires a matching encoder: transformers for text, ViTs or CNNs for images, wav2vec / Whisper-style encoders for audio. In multimodal models, each encoder maps its modality into a shared embedding space so a single LLM backbone can reason across them. New modalities — 3D point clouds, molecular graphs, time-series — continue to be added via new encoders rather than retraining from scratch.
why_it_matters: Choosing which modalities your product needs decides your model selection, data pipeline, and cost structure.

title: Text Generation
short_definition: The task of producing natural-language output from a model, one token at a time, conditioned on a prompt or context.
full_explanation: Text generation is the core task modern LLMs are trained for. Given a prompt, the model produces a probability distribution over next tokens, a sampler picks one (greedy, top-p, temperature), it gets appended to the context, and the loop repeats. Applications span chat, summarization, translation, code completion, and creative writing. Quality depends on base model capability, prompt design, and sampling configuration — all three are tunable.
why_it_matters: Every chat app, code assistant, and content tool is text generation under the hood. It is the workload most inference infrastructure is optimized for.

title: Summarization
short_definition: The task of producing a shorter version of a document that preserves the key information.
full_explanation: Summarization comes in two styles: extractive (select spans from the source) and abstractive (generate new phrasing). Modern LLMs do abstractive summarization zero-shot from a prompt like 'summarize the following in 3 bullets.' Evaluation is hard — ROUGE scores correlate poorly with quality, so human ratings or LLM-as-judge are used. Failure modes: hallucination (adding facts not in source), omission (dropping critical points), and genre drift (news summary written like marketing copy).
why_it_matters: Summarization is the most common production LLM workload after chat — meeting notes, document digests, ticket triage, email threads.
common_misconceptions: Longer summaries are not always more accurate. Faithful summarization is about selection, not elaboration.

title: Translation (Machine Translation)
short_definition: The task of converting text or speech from one language to another, preserving meaning.
full_explanation: Neural machine translation used to require specialized encoder-decoder models (seq2seq, MarianMT, NLLB). Frontier LLMs now match or beat these in most language pairs, with strong zero-shot translation between hundreds of languages. Evaluation uses BLEU (older, n-gram overlap), chrF, COMET (learned metric, better correlation with humans). Open challenges: low-resource languages, idiom preservation, and keeping style and register consistent across documents.
why_it_matters: Translation is the dominant multilingual workload. LLMs replaced most legacy MT systems for high-resource languages between 2022 and 2025.

title: Named Entity Recognition
short_definition: An NLP task that identifies and classifies spans of text into categories like person, organization, location, date, or money.
full_explanation: NER was historically solved with CRF-tagged BiLSTMs and later BERT-based token classifiers. LLMs now do zero- or few-shot NER via prompts and structured output. Evaluation uses span-level precision and recall — partial-match scoring (type-match only vs. type + boundary match) is a frequent point of confusion. Domain-specific entities (drug names, legal citations) still benefit from fine-tuning a smaller model when throughput or cost dominates.
why_it_matters: NER is the foundation of information extraction, redaction, and many search pipelines. It is often a first step before downstream reasoning.

title: Sentiment Analysis
short_definition: Classifying text by emotional tone — typically positive, negative, neutral — or by more fine-grained emotions or aspects.
full_explanation: Classic sentiment analysis uses fine-tuned BERT on labeled reviews. Modern LLMs do it zero-shot. Extensions: aspect-based sentiment (what part of the product is loved or hated), fine-grained emotion (anger, fear, joy), stance detection (for/against a claim). Pitfalls: sarcasm, domain shift (product reviews differ from clinical notes), and confounding variables — users who complain loudest may not represent the average.
why_it_matters: Sentiment feeds trend detection, customer-experience dashboards, and content moderation. It is the go-to example for 'is NLP working here?'
common_misconceptions: Sentiment models often conflate negativity with anger or disappointment. Fine-grained emotion is a different task.

title: Question Answering
short_definition: The task of producing an answer to a natural-language question, either from a passage, a knowledge base, or world knowledge.
full_explanation: QA has three main flavors: extractive (pick a span from a given passage — SQuAD), closed-book (answer from parameters alone), open-domain (retrieve then answer — RAG). Modern assistants are open-domain QA systems at heart. Evaluation uses exact match, F1 for spans, and LLM-as-judge for free-form. The hardest QA challenges involve multi-hop reasoning, citation faithfulness, and refusing when the answer is unknown.
why_it_matters: QA is the north-star task for chatbots, docs search, and enterprise knowledge assistants. Most RAG projects are QA projects.

title: Data Augmentation
short_definition: Techniques for expanding a training dataset by generating modified copies of existing examples without changing their labels.
full_explanation: In vision, augmentation means random crops, flips, color jitter, and more aggressive techniques like CutMix and RandAugment. In NLP, augmentation is trickier because small edits can flip labels — back-translation, synonym replacement, and entity swapping are safer. For LLMs, synthetic data generation (prompt a stronger model to paraphrase or extend examples) is the modern augmentation. Augmentation is a form of regularization — it encourages invariance to irrelevant variations.
why_it_matters: Augmentation often gives a bigger accuracy bump than tuning the model. For small datasets it is sometimes the difference between shippable and not.
common_misconceptions: Too-aggressive augmentation distorts the label distribution and hurts quality. Always visually or textually inspect augmented samples.

title: Feature Engineering
short_definition: The craft of transforming raw data into features that expose the structure a model needs — dominant in tabular ML, largely replaced by learned features in deep learning.
full_explanation: In classical ML, feature engineering was the lion's share of work: discretization, one-hot encoding, log transforms, interaction features, time-based features, target encoding. Good features could make a linear model beat a deep network. In deep learning, the model learns features from raw inputs, rendering manual crafting mostly obsolete for vision and language. Tabular data remains the last stronghold where feature engineering plus gradient-boosted trees still often beats deep models.
why_it_matters: If your task is tabular (tabular risk scoring, forecasting, recommendations), mastering feature engineering beats mastering neural architecture.

title: Prefix Tuning
short_definition: A parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that prepends a small set of trainable vectors to every transformer layer's key/value cache while freezing the base model.
full_explanation: Prefix tuning (Li & Liang 2021) learns continuous task-specific 'prefix' vectors that are prepended to the keys and values at every attention layer. The base model is frozen; only the prefix (typically 0.1–1% of total parameters) is trained. It preceded LoRA and is conceptually similar — both steer attention with small added parameters. Prefix tuning is still used in production where adapter-style modifications are awkward, and it inspired the broader family of soft-prompt methods.
why_it_matters: Prefix tuning proved you do not need to touch model weights to specialize behavior — foundational insight for PEFT.
common_misconceptions: Prefix tuning is not the same as prompt tuning; prefix tuning touches every layer, prompt tuning only touches the input.

title: Prompt Tuning
short_definition: Fine-tuning only a small set of continuous vectors prepended to the input tokens, leaving the base model completely frozen.
full_explanation: Prompt tuning (Lester et al. 2021) is the simplest PEFT method: learn N 'virtual' embedding vectors, prepend them to the tokenized input, and train only those vectors on your task. No modification to any transformer layer. Works surprisingly well at 10B+ scale but underperforms LoRA on smaller models. The 'soft prompt' is not human-readable — it exists only in embedding space.
why_it_matters: Prompt tuning showed that a handful of learned vectors can specialize a frozen 10B+ model — a precursor to modern PEFT.

title: Adapter (PEFT Adapter)
short_definition: Small neural modules inserted inside a frozen base model's layers; only the adapter weights are trained.
full_explanation: Adapter modules (Houlsby et al. 2019) are bottleneck MLPs inserted after the attention or feed-forward block of each transformer layer. A down-projection, nonlinearity, up-projection, and residual — typically 0.5–5% of total parameters. Different adapters can be swapped at inference time to switch tasks without reloading the base model. LoRA is a specific, simpler variant where the adapter is a low-rank linear update merged into existing weights.
why_it_matters: Adapters predate and underpin LoRA. Multi-adapter serving is how some providers host thousands of fine-tunes on a single base model.

title: PEFT (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning)
short_definition: The umbrella term for fine-tuning methods that update only a tiny fraction of a model's parameters — LoRA, adapters, prefix tuning, IA3 — to save memory and compute.
full_explanation: PEFT methods freeze the base model and train small added or selected parameters. Benefits: 10–100× less VRAM, fast training, easy adapter swapping, and multiple task-specific tunes sharing a single base model in memory. The dominant PEFT method is LoRA and its quantized variant QLoRA. Hugging Face's PEFT library is the de facto standard implementation, supporting LoRA, DoRA, IA3, adapters, prompt tuning, and more under a unified API.
why_it_matters: PEFT made fine-tuning democratic — a single developer with one GPU can now adapt 70B models.

title: IA3 (Infused Adapter by Inhibiting and Amplifying Inner Activations)
short_definition: A PEFT method that rescales activations with learned per-channel multiplicative vectors — extremely few trainable parameters.
full_explanation: IA3 (Liu et al. 2022) introduces three learned vectors per transformer block: one that scales the keys, one the values, and one the intermediate MLP activations. The vectors are initialized to ones (identity) and updated during fine-tuning. Trainable parameters are roughly 0.01% of the model — far fewer than LoRA — yet performance on many tasks is competitive. Trade-off: less expressive than LoRA for complex tasks.
why_it_matters: IA3 is the cheapest PEFT method on disk. Useful for serving many specialized variants of the same base with negligible storage.

title: DoRA (Weight-Decomposed Low-Rank Adaptation)
short_definition: A LoRA variant that decomposes weight updates into magnitude and direction components, consistently matching or beating LoRA.
full_explanation: DoRA (Liu et al. 2024) decomposes each pretrained weight matrix into a magnitude vector and a directional matrix, then applies LoRA only to the directional component while learning magnitude separately. The split mirrors how full fine-tuning changes weights — magnitudes shift more than directions — and closes the gap between LoRA and full fine-tuning on many benchmarks without extra inference cost after merging.
why_it_matters: DoRA is a near drop-in upgrade to LoRA with small quality gains and similar training cost.

title: Full Fine-Tuning
short_definition: Updating every parameter in a pretrained model during fine-tuning — maximum expressiveness at maximum cost.
full_explanation: Full fine-tuning applies standard gradient descent to all parameters of the base model. For a 70B model this means ~500GB of optimizer state (Adam stores momentum and variance per parameter), demanding multi-GPU setups. The result can be marginally better than LoRA for large training sets or tasks that shift model behavior broadly, but for most practical work LoRA/QLoRA hits within 1% of full fine-tuning quality at a fraction of cost.
why_it_matters: Full fine-tuning is the baseline you compare PEFT methods against. Rarely justified given LoRA's quality/cost ratio.
common_misconceptions: Full fine-tuning is not automatically better — it can overfit more readily and destroy useful pretrained behavior if not regularized.

title: Continued Pretraining
short_definition: Extending the pretraining phase of a base model on domain-specific raw text, before any instruction tuning.
full_explanation: Continued pretraining (also called domain adaptive pretraining or DAPT) runs causal language modeling on new unlabeled domain data (legal, medical, financial, a company's codebase) on top of an existing base model. Token counts range from hundreds of millions to tens of billions. Low learning rates and partial data replay prevent catastrophic forgetting of general knowledge. It is the most effective way to inject large amounts of domain knowledge into a model's weights.
why_it_matters: When RAG hits its limits or domain jargon is dense, continued pretraining is the tool that moves knowledge from retrieval to parameters.
common_misconceptions: It is not the same as fine-tuning. Continued pretraining uses raw text; fine-tuning uses task-formatted examples.

title: Self-Instruct
short_definition: A method for bootstrapping instruction datasets by having an LLM generate new instructions and responses from a small seed set.
full_explanation: Self-Instruct (Wang et al. 2022) starts with roughly 175 human-written seed instructions, prompts a strong LLM to generate new instructions in the same style, generates responses, and filters low-quality outputs via heuristics. The Stanford Alpaca dataset was built this way using GPT-3. The technique made instruction tuning tractable without large human annotation budgets but inherits the teacher model's biases and license constraints.
why_it_matters: Self-Instruct kicked off the open-source instruction-tuning wave. Most small-lab instruct datasets are descendants of this recipe.
common_misconceptions: Generated data often inherits the teacher's style and failure modes — always hand-inspect samples.

title: Evol-Instruct
short_definition: A synthetic instruction generation method that iteratively rewrites simple instructions into harder ones via explicit evolution operators.
full_explanation: Evol-Instruct (WizardLM, Xu et al. 2023) takes seed instructions and prompts an LLM to apply transformations: add constraints, deepen reasoning, complicate input, increase breadth, concretize. Each generation produces harder instructions from which high-quality responses are then generated. The result is a dataset skewed toward complex multi-step tasks that simpler synthetic pipelines miss. Used in WizardLM, WizardCoder, and Orca-style distillation.
why_it_matters: Evol-Instruct proved that synthetic data complexity — not just quantity — is a key driver of instruction-tuned model quality.

title: Rejection Sampling (Training)
short_definition: A post-training technique where the model generates many candidates per prompt, a reward model or verifier picks the best, and only those are used for SFT.
full_explanation: Rejection sampling fine-tuning (RFT) is an RL-free way to lift model quality. Sample N completions per prompt from the current model, score them with a reward model or an external verifier (unit tests for code, checker for math), keep the top-k per prompt, and fine-tune on the filtered dataset. Llama 3 and DeepSeek-R1 use rejection sampling iteratively — a cheap alternative to full RLHF that still uses the signal from preference data.
why_it_matters: Rejection sampling captures much of RLHF's benefit with standard SFT tooling. It is the practical workhorse for many post-training pipelines.

title: Best-of-N Sampling
short_definition: An inference-time strategy where the model generates N candidates and a reward model or verifier picks the best.
full_explanation: Best-of-N (BoN) trades inference compute for quality. Sample N completions at nonzero temperature, score each with a reward model, return the top one. Quality scales roughly as log(N) for a fixed reward model — gains saturate around N=16 to 64. Works especially well when the reward model is cheap relative to generation (e.g., small classifier vs. large LLM) or when the task has a checkable oracle (tests, math verifier).
why_it_matters: BoN is the cheapest inference-time quality lift available. It underpins many 'test-time compute' techniques behind reasoning models.
code_example:
cands = [llm.complete(prompt, temperature=0.8) for _ in range(16)]
scores = [reward_model(prompt, c) for c in cands]
return cands[scores.index(max(scores))]

title: PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization)
short_definition: The RL algorithm historically used in RLHF to fine-tune an LLM against a reward model while constraining how far the policy drifts from the base.
full_explanation: PPO (Schulman et al. 2017) is a policy-gradient method that clips the ratio of new-policy to old-policy action probabilities, preventing destructive updates. In RLHF, PPO uses a learned reward model to produce per-token or per-response rewards, with a KL-divergence penalty keeping the fine-tuned policy near the SFT starting point. PPO works but is notoriously finicky — reward hacking, KL explosions, and actor-critic instabilities consume entire teams. DPO and ORPO largely replaced it in open-source post-training.
why_it_matters: PPO was the engine of ChatGPT-era RLHF. Understanding it is key to understanding why simpler methods like DPO took over.

title: KTO (Kahneman-Tversky Optimization)
short_definition: A preference-optimization method that trains on individual positive or negative signals rather than paired preferences, inspired by prospect theory.
full_explanation: KTO (Ethayarajh et al. 2024) removes the need for paired (chosen, rejected) data. Each example is labeled simply as 'desirable' or 'undesirable.' The loss is derived from Kahneman-Tversky prospect theory — humans weight losses more than gains. KTO performs comparably to DPO while being compatible with thumbs-up/thumbs-down feedback that accumulates naturally in production, where collecting strict pairwise comparisons is expensive.
why_it_matters: KTO is a production-friendly alternative to DPO when you have binary feedback but not preference pairs.

title: ORPO (Odds Ratio Preference Optimization)
short_definition: A single-stage fine-tuning method that combines SFT and preference learning in one loss, eliminating the need for a separate DPO stage.
full_explanation: ORPO (Hong et al. 2024) adds an odds-ratio preference term to the SFT loss so the model learns to follow instructions AND prefer chosen over rejected responses in one training run. No reference model is needed, cutting memory use roughly in half compared to DPO. Results on common benchmarks are competitive with or exceed the traditional SFT→DPO pipeline.
why_it_matters: ORPO simplifies post-training: one stage, one loss, no reference model. It is increasingly the default for small-lab alignment pipelines.

title: SimPO
short_definition: A reference-free preference optimization method with length-normalized reward, consistently matching or beating DPO at lower memory cost.
full_explanation: SimPO (Meng et al. 2024) drops the reference model from DPO entirely — its reward is the average log probability of the response under the current policy, length-normalized to avoid length bias. A target reward margin keeps training stable. Memory footprint roughly halves, and empirical results on Arena, AlpacaEval 2, and MT-Bench exceed DPO for many base models. A popular modern choice for the preference-optimization stage.
why_it_matters: SimPO is faster, cheaper, and often better than DPO. It is a strong default for the preference-optimization stage.

title: Constitutional AI
short_definition: An alignment method from Anthropic where an AI critiques and revises its own responses against a written set of principles, reducing the need for human red-teamers.
full_explanation: Constitutional AI (Bai et al. 2022) uses a two-stage process. First, the model generates a response, then critiques it against principles ('be helpful, avoid harmful advice, be honest about uncertainty') and produces a revised response. This creates synthetic preference pairs without human labelers. Second, RLAIF (RL from AI feedback) uses an AI preference model derived from the constitution to do RL. Claude is trained with constitutional techniques.
why_it_matters: Constitutional AI scales safety training by removing the human-labeler bottleneck on harmful-content preferences.

title: RLAIF (Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback)
short_definition: RLHF where an LLM, rather than a human, produces the preference labels used to train the reward model.
full_explanation: RLAIF (Lee et al. 2023) replaces human annotators with a capable LLM that compares pairs of responses and picks the better one. The LLM's preferences train a reward model, which then drives RL fine-tuning. Quality nearly matches RLHF on helpfulness and harmlessness benchmarks, at a fraction of the annotation cost. RLAIF is the default for aligning open-source instruct models at small labs.
why_it_matters: RLAIF sidesteps the biggest cost of RLHF — human labelers — without a significant quality hit for most tasks.
common_misconceptions: RLAIF is not free of human input — the LLM judge's preferences reflect whatever trained it. Biases propagate.

title: Process Reward Model
short_definition: A reward model that scores each intermediate step of a reasoning chain, not just the final answer — used to train and guide reasoning models.
full_explanation: Process reward models (PRMs) score the correctness of each reasoning step independently. Outcome reward models (ORMs) only score the final answer. PRMs give denser training signal for math and code — a model can get partial credit for the first 5 correct steps even if it fails on step 6. OpenAI's 'Let's Verify Step by Step' (2023) and Anthropic's o1-scale reasoning research have shown PRMs substantially outperform ORMs on hard reasoning benchmarks like MATH.
why_it_matters: PRMs are the engine behind the current reasoning-model wave. They let RL exploit every reasoning step, not just the end.

title: Outcome Reward Model
short_definition: A reward model that scores only the final answer of a generation, not the intermediate steps.
full_explanation: Outcome reward models (ORMs) are the simpler counterpart to process reward models. They train on final-answer correctness, making them cheap to build — a binary classifier over (prompt, final answer) pairs. ORMs work well for tasks with clear end-state correctness but give little training signal on reasoning quality. Most RLHF reward models are ORMs. For tasks where multiple paths lead to the same answer, ORMs can reinforce flawed reasoning that happened to hit the right number.
why_it_matters: ORMs are cheaper and simpler but coarser. PRMs vs. ORMs is an active design trade-off in reasoning-model training.

title: Speculative Decoding
short_definition: An inference speedup where a small fast 'draft' model predicts several tokens ahead, which the large target model then verifies in parallel.
full_explanation: Speculative decoding (Leviathan et al. 2023, Chen et al. 2023) uses a small draft model to propose k tokens. The large target model runs one forward pass that scores all k proposed tokens at once. Matching tokens are accepted; the first mismatch is corrected by sampling from the target's distribution. When the draft agrees often, you get multi-token throughput per forward pass. Speedups of 2–3× are routine with minimal quality impact.
why_it_matters: Speculative decoding is the single biggest inference-latency optimization at zero quality cost. Built into vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, and SGLang.
common_misconceptions: Speculative decoding does not reduce total FLOPs — it increases them. It saves wall-clock time by amortizing memory-bound latency.

title: Medusa Decoding
short_definition: A self-speculative decoding technique that adds multiple prediction heads to a single model, removing the need for a separate draft model.
full_explanation: Medusa (Cai et al. 2024) attaches K extra decoding heads to the target model. Each head predicts tokens at position t+1, t+2, ..., t+K from a single forward pass. The predicted sequences form a tree-structured candidate set that is verified in parallel with tree attention. End-to-end speedups of 2–3× without a separate draft model. Training the Medusa heads is lightweight (a few hours of LoRA-style fine-tuning).
why_it_matters: Medusa delivers speculative-decoding gains without the operational complexity of running two models. Increasingly common in production serving.

title: Lookahead Decoding
short_definition: A draft-model-free decoding speedup that uses Jacobi iteration to predict multiple tokens in parallel.
full_explanation: Lookahead decoding (Fu et al. 2024) maintains an N-gram pool of candidate continuations. At each step, it uses Jacobi iteration — fixed-point parallel updates — to refine the token sequence, verifying candidates against the true model in parallel. Unlike Medusa, no architecture changes or training are needed. Speedups are typically 1.5–2× and compose with other optimizations.
why_it_matters: Lookahead decoding is a free speedup — no new heads, no draft model, no retraining. Pure inference-time win.

title: Model Merging (Model Souping)
short_definition: Combining the weights of multiple fine-tuned models into one by averaging or more sophisticated blending, often improving quality for free.
full_explanation: Model merging blends the weights of models fine-tuned from the same base. Simple averaging ('model soup') works when the tunes are in the same loss basin. SLERP (spherical linear interpolation), TIES-Merging, and DARE handle conflicting updates by pruning low-magnitude changes. Merging costs nothing at training time — you get a better model by blending existing ones. Many top-ranked open-source models on Arena are merges of prior fine-tunes.
why_it_matters: Merging gives free quality gains if you already have multiple fine-tunes. It is the cheapest post-training lever available.
common_misconceptions: Merging only works between models from the same pretraining checkpoint. Merging across base models usually produces broken weights.

title: SLERP (Spherical Linear Interpolation)
short_definition: A weight-blending method for model merging that interpolates along a sphere rather than linearly, preserving vector norms.
full_explanation: SLERP was originally developed for 3D animation. For model merging it interpolates between two sets of weights along the shortest arc of the unit sphere, preserving the norm of the weight vectors. Empirically, SLERP merges often outperform simple weighted averages when the parent models are meaningfully different. MergeKit implements SLERP as the default for many community merges.
why_it_matters: SLERP is the most common merging method in open-source LLM cooking. Many Open LLM Leaderboard entries are SLERP blends.

title: TIES-Merging
short_definition: A model merging method that resolves conflicts by trimming small-magnitude changes, electing signs, and disjoint-merging what remains.
full_explanation: TIES (TRIM, ELECT, DISJOINT MERGE) is a three-step algorithm for combining multiple task-specific fine-tunes. It first trims each task vector to keep only top-k% magnitude parameters, then resolves sign conflicts by taking the majority direction, then averages only the parameters whose signs agreed. The process preserves each model's most important updates while cleanly combining non-conflicting knowledge.
why_it_matters: TIES is one of the most robust merge recipes when you are combining 3+ specialized models into one generalist.

title: DARE (Drop And REscale)
short_definition: A model merging method that randomly drops parameters from task vectors and rescales survivors, producing sparse merges.
full_explanation: DARE (Yu et al. 2023) randomly zeros out a large fraction of each task vector's parameters (typically 90%) and rescales the rest by 1/(1-drop_rate). The surprising finding: most fine-tuning parameter changes are redundant, so dropping 90% and keeping 10% retains almost all task performance. DARE combines cleanly with TIES — use DARE first to densify, then TIES to merge.
why_it_matters: DARE exposes how sparse real task-learning is. Practical benefit: merges stay stable as you add more models.

title: Knowledge Editing
short_definition: Surgically updating specific facts inside a trained model's weights without full fine-tuning — e.g., changing what it says the capital of a country is.
full_explanation: Knowledge editing methods like ROME (Meng et al. 2022) and MEMIT localize factual knowledge to specific MLP layers, then compute targeted rank-one or rank-batch weight updates that shift a specific (subject, relation, object) triple. You can change 'The capital of France is Paris' to some alternative and the model updates without broader behavior drift. Limitations: edits can ripple and break adjacent facts, especially at scale past a few thousand edits.
why_it_matters: For scenarios where RAG is too slow and retraining is too expensive, knowledge editing is a surgical alternative.
common_misconceptions: Edits are not permanent in the social-verification sense — the model can still retrieve old facts via related questions.

title: ROME (Rank-One Model Editing)
short_definition: A knowledge-editing algorithm that changes a single fact inside an LLM via a rank-one update to a specific MLP layer's output matrix.
full_explanation: ROME identifies the transformer layer where a given fact is 'stored' (typically a mid-to-late feed-forward block), computes a rank-one update to that layer's W_out matrix so the subject's representation maps to a new object, and applies it. Single-fact edits complete in seconds and preserve most unrelated behavior. The approach is limited to one edit at a time — MEMIT extends it to batch edits.
why_it_matters: ROME demonstrated that factual knowledge in LLMs has addressable locations — a foundational result for interpretability and editability.

title: MEMIT (Mass-Editing Memory in a Transformer)
short_definition: An extension of ROME that edits thousands of facts simultaneously by distributing updates across multiple MLP layers.
full_explanation: MEMIT (Meng et al. 2022) solves a constrained least-squares problem to update multiple MLP output weights so many (subject, relation, object) facts hold at once. Batch sizes of 10,000+ edits with limited degradation are feasible. Side effects remain the main limitation: adjacent facts and analogical reasoning patterns can shift unexpectedly.
why_it_matters: MEMIT made batch fact editing viable, opening the door to periodic model 'patches' without retraining.

title: Activation Steering
short_definition: Modifying an LLM's residual stream activations at inference time to steer its behavior — e.g., making it more truthful, safer, or more on-topic.
full_explanation: Activation steering works by computing a 'steering vector' — the mean activation difference between behavior A and behavior B at a chosen layer — then adding that vector to the residual stream at inference time. Anthropic's activation-additions and representation engineering (Zou et al. 2023) showed steering can increase truthfulness, reduce jailbreak success, and enforce persona without any training. Effects are surgical but brittle — strong steering can distort unrelated outputs.
why_it_matters: Activation steering is the fastest way to influence LLM behavior — no training, no fine-tuning, just an inference-time vector add.

title: Sparse Autoencoders (Interpretability)
short_definition: Autoencoders trained on LLM activations with a sparsity penalty, used to decompose dense activations into interpretable features.
full_explanation: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) for interpretability train a wide, over-complete encoder on a layer's activations with an L1 penalty on the hidden state, forcing most features to be off for any given input. The resulting features often correspond to human-interpretable concepts — 'French language,' 'legal disclaimers,' 'bridges.' Anthropic's 'Scaling Monosemanticity' (2024) trained SAEs on Claude 3 Sonnet and identified millions of features. SAEs are a major tool in mechanistic interpretability.
why_it_matters: SAEs give us a way to look inside the black box. They are central to current interpretability and safety research.

title: Mechanistic Interpretability
short_definition: The research program of reverse-engineering neural networks into human-understandable algorithms, circuit by circuit.
full_explanation: Mechanistic interpretability (mech interp) treats trained networks as compiled programs and tries to disassemble them. Circuits research (Anthropic's Transformer Circuits thread) identifies specific attention heads and MLP neurons that implement identifiable algorithms — induction heads for in-context learning, copy-suppression heads, indirect-object identification circuits. Tools: activation patching, causal tracing, sparse autoencoders, attribution patching, direct logit attribution.
why_it_matters: Mech interp is the most serious attempt to audit what LLMs actually do. It is foundational for safety certification and debugging.
common_misconceptions: Mech interp findings are usually local and probabilistic — a circuit that 'does' a task is one pathway among many, not the model's sole mechanism.

title: Probing
short_definition: An interpretability method that trains a small classifier on a frozen model's activations to test what information is linearly encoded.
full_explanation: Probing trains a lightweight classifier (typically linear) on activations from a specific layer to predict a property — 'does this token represent a noun?', 'does this residual stream encode sentiment?'. High probe accuracy implies the information is present and linearly decodable. Probing is a blunt tool — presence does not imply use — but it is cheap and historically foundational to interpretability.
why_it_matters: Probing gives a first-order map of what a layer 'knows' without the complexity of causal interventions.
common_misconceptions: Probing measures decodability, not causal relevance. A model may encode something and never use it in its computation.

title: Distillation (Knowledge Distillation, Reprise)
short_definition: Training a smaller 'student' model to match a larger 'teacher' model's outputs — trading capacity for speed at similar quality.
full_explanation: Distillation extends beyond the classic concept of knowledge distillation. Variants: logit distillation (match teacher probabilities), data distillation (teacher generates training data for the student), feature distillation (match hidden states). LLM distillation is mostly data distillation — a strong teacher generates high-quality responses that the student learns to imitate via SFT. Gemini Nano, Phi-3, and Llama-3.2-1B are distillation products.
why_it_matters: Distillation is the main way to ship capable small models. It is how frontier-quality behavior reaches phones and the edge.

title: Self-Distillation
short_definition: Distillation where the teacher and student are the same model size — often the same model at different checkpoints or ensembling with itself.
full_explanation: Self-distillation trains a model to match its own outputs, typically from a stronger prompt or configuration. Common recipe: use chain-of-thought to generate answers, distill the model on (prompt → final answer) pairs so it produces answers directly without the chain, compressing inference. DeepSeek-R1-Distill is a high-profile example: distill R1's reasoning traces into smaller Qwen/Llama students.
why_it_matters: Self-distillation compresses expensive inference patterns into cheaper direct generation with minimal quality loss.

title: Gradient Accumulation
short_definition: Summing gradients over multiple small micro-batches before applying a single optimizer step, emulating a large batch on limited-memory hardware.
full_explanation: Training with a global batch size of 1024 on a GPU that can only fit 16 examples requires gradient accumulation. You process 64 micro-batches of 16, sum gradients, then call optimizer.step() and zero out. Loss is averaged — if you do not scale correctly you can under- or over-weight updates. All modern training frameworks (Trainer, Accelerate, DeepSpeed, FSDP) expose an accumulation_steps parameter.
why_it_matters: Gradient accumulation is how small teams match papers' batch sizes without matching their hardware.
common_misconceptions: Gradient accumulation does not literally match large-batch training — batch norm statistics and learning-rate schedules can differ subtly.

title: Gradient Checkpointing
short_definition: A memory-saving technique that recomputes activations during the backward pass instead of storing them, trading compute for VRAM.
full_explanation: Normally, forward activations are stored for use in backprop. Gradient checkpointing stores only a sparse set (e.g., every N-th layer) and recomputes intermediate activations as needed during backward. Memory drops by roughly √N; training is roughly 30% slower per step. Enabled by `model.gradient_checkpointing_enable()` in Transformers. Combined with mixed precision and FSDP/DeepSpeed ZeRO, it lets you fit much bigger models on the same hardware.
why_it_matters: Gradient checkpointing is the difference between an out-of-memory error and a successful training run on constrained GPUs.

title: Mixed Precision Training
short_definition: Training with some tensors in low precision (fp16 or bf16) and others in fp32, roughly halving memory and doubling throughput.
full_explanation: Mixed precision keeps weights and optimizer state in fp32 but casts activations and gradients to fp16 (legacy) or bf16 (modern). Tensor Cores on Nvidia GPUs accelerate fp16/bf16 matmul 2–4×. BF16 preserves fp32's dynamic range, avoiding the overflow issues that plagued fp16 training and needed gradient loss-scaling workarounds. BF16 is now default on A100/H100; older V100s still use fp16 with loss-scaling.
why_it_matters: Mixed precision is free speed and memory. Essentially every modern LLM training run uses it.

title: FP8 Training
short_definition: Training with 8-bit floating-point precision on supported hardware (H100+), doubling throughput over bf16 with careful scaling.
full_explanation: FP8 has two formats: E4M3 (more precision, less range) for forward activations and E5M2 (more range) for gradients. Hardware support arrived with Nvidia Hopper (H100). FP8 training requires per-tensor scaling factors, careful choice of which ops to run in FP8 vs. bf16, and often a hybrid approach. Nvidia's Transformer Engine and MS-AMP are the dominant libraries. Recipes show ~2× throughput over bf16 with matching quality.
why_it_matters: FP8 is the current frontier for frontier-model training. Many 2026-era training runs use FP8 for the matmul-heavy parts.

title: FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel)
short_definition: PyTorch's native technique for sharding model parameters, gradients, and optimizer state across GPUs to train models that do not fit on one device.
full_explanation: FSDP shards the three memory-heavy components (parameters, gradients, optimizer state) across all data-parallel workers. At forward time, each worker gathers the shards it needs for its layer, computes, and discards the non-owned shards. Mimics DeepSpeed ZeRO-3 with tighter PyTorch integration. FSDP-2 introduces per-parameter sharding for finer control and better composability with tensor parallelism.
why_it_matters: FSDP is the default distributed-training API in PyTorch. It is how you train 70B+ models across clusters without custom frameworks.

title: DeepSpeed ZeRO
short_definition: Microsoft's memory-optimization technique that shards optimizer state (ZeRO-1), gradients (ZeRO-2), and parameters (ZeRO-3) across GPUs.
full_explanation: ZeRO (Zero Redundancy Optimizer) is a family of partitioning strategies for distributed training. ZeRO-1 partitions optimizer state only; ZeRO-2 adds gradient partitioning; ZeRO-3 partitions parameters too, matching FSDP. ZeRO-Infinity extends partitioning to CPU and NVMe offload, enabling trillion-parameter training on modest clusters at significant throughput cost. DeepSpeed was the original; many open-source labs now prefer FSDP for its native PyTorch integration.
why_it_matters: DeepSpeed ZeRO was the first approach to make training huge models on commodity clusters practical.

title: Tensor Parallelism
short_definition: Splitting individual matrix multiplications across multiple GPUs so each GPU computes a shard of the output.
full_explanation: Tensor parallelism (Megatron-style) shards weight matrices along rows or columns. A matmul Y = XW can be split: each GPU holds a slice of W, computes a partial output, then gather or all-reduce merges the results. Intra-layer parallelism is communication-heavy and benefits from NVLink. For large models like 70B+, TP is combined with pipeline parallelism (across layers) and data parallelism (across batches) — 3D parallelism.
why_it_matters: Tensor parallelism is how a single forward pass runs across multiple GPUs when the model weights do not fit on one.

title: Pipeline Parallelism
short_definition: Splitting a model across multiple GPUs by assigning consecutive layers to different devices, streaming micro-batches through the pipeline.
full_explanation: Pipeline parallelism partitions the model vertically — layers 1–8 on GPU 0, layers 9–16 on GPU 1, and so on. During a step, micro-batches flow through the pipeline, overlapping forward and backward passes to minimize GPU idle time. Schemes: GPipe (fill-then-drain, simpler), 1F1B (interleaved, lower bubble), zero bubble pipelines (newer). Pipeline parallelism has lower communication per step than tensor parallelism but introduces pipeline 'bubbles' that waste compute.
why_it_matters: Pipeline parallelism crosses node boundaries over slower interconnects, unlike TP which demands NVLink. It is key for training on multi-node clusters.

title: Data Parallelism
short_definition: The simplest distributed training: each GPU holds a full copy of the model and processes different shards of the batch, then gradients are averaged.
full_explanation: Standard data parallelism (DP or DDP in PyTorch) replicates the full model on every GPU and uses all-reduce to average gradients after each backward pass. Scales linearly with GPU count up to communication bottleneck (tens of GPUs on Ethernet, hundreds on InfiniBand). DP is the default for models that fit on a single device; FSDP or ZeRO kicks in when they do not.
why_it_matters: DDP is the starter distribution pattern. Most fine-tuning runs use it without needing anything more exotic.

title: 3D Parallelism
short_definition: Combining data parallelism, tensor parallelism, and pipeline parallelism to train frontier-scale models across clusters of GPUs.
full_explanation: 3D parallelism arranges GPUs in a 3D grid. Within a node, tensor parallelism shards matmuls across GPUs with fast NVLink. Pipeline parallelism spans nodes with slower InfiniBand. Data parallelism replicates the whole setup. Megatron-LM and DeepSpeed are the reference implementations. Training GPT-3 required 3D parallelism across thousands of GPUs; Llama 3 405B used similar techniques.
why_it_matters: 3D parallelism is how frontier labs scale to 1T+ parameter runs. It is the compositional core of modern training infrastructure.

title: Pruning (Neural Network Pruning)
short_definition: Removing weights from a trained model to shrink it — either unstructured (zero individual weights) or structured (remove whole heads, channels, or layers).
full_explanation: Pruning exploits the observation that most weights in overparameterized models are close to zero and contribute little. Magnitude pruning zeros out the smallest weights; structured pruning removes entire attention heads, MLP neurons, or transformer layers. Unstructured pruning yields sparse matrices that need specialized kernels (like Nvidia's 2:4 sparsity) to accelerate; structured pruning is drop-in faster. Typically followed by fine-tuning to recover lost quality.
why_it_matters: Pruning shrinks models for edge deployment or cheaper serving. Often composed with quantization for multiplicative gains.

title: Structured Pruning
short_definition: Pruning that removes entire architectural units — heads, channels, layers — producing a smaller dense model that runs faster on any hardware.
full_explanation: Structured pruning removes whole attention heads, MLP channels, or transformer layers by measuring their importance (via loss-based scores, activation magnitudes, or Taylor expansions). The pruned model stays dense and standard — unlike unstructured pruning, it needs no custom sparse kernels. Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-Pruned and SliceGPT-style layer dropping are examples. Followed by a short distillation phase to recover quality.
why_it_matters: Structured pruning is the easiest way to speed up inference without changing your serving stack.

title: GPTQ (Post-Training Quantization)
short_definition: A post-training quantization method that reduces LLM weights to 3–4 bits using layer-wise error minimization — a widely used production quantizer.
full_explanation: GPTQ (Frantar et al. 2023) quantizes one layer at a time, solving a layer-level optimization that minimizes the output reconstruction error on a small calibration set. It handles outlier weights better than round-to-nearest and produces models whose generation quality matches the full-precision original on most tasks. Widely supported by vLLM, Transformers, and inference servers. GPTQ and AWQ are the two dominant 4-bit LLM quantizers.
why_it_matters: GPTQ makes running 70B models on a single consumer GPU routine. Most 4-bit open-source checkpoints use it or AWQ.

title: AWQ (Activation-Aware Weight Quantization)
short_definition: A 4-bit weight-only quantization method that scales weights based on their corresponding activation magnitudes, protecting important channels from quantization error.
full_explanation: AWQ (Lin et al. 2023) observes that only a small fraction of weight channels — those that consume large activations — matter for quality. It scales these channels up before quantization and scales the corresponding activations down, keeping key channels in a numerically friendly range. The result is 4-bit quantization with near-zero quality loss, often outperforming GPTQ on instruct models. TensorRT-LLM and vLLM both support AWQ kernels.
why_it_matters: AWQ is the modern default for 4-bit LLM serving. It often wins over GPTQ on latency and quality jointly.

title: GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer)
short_definition: The decoder-only transformer architecture and OpenAI model family that established modern autoregressive LLMs as the dominant paradigm.
full_explanation: GPT refers both to the architecture (decoder-only transformer trained with causal language modeling) and to OpenAI's specific model series — GPT-2 (2019), GPT-3 (2020), GPT-3.5/ChatGPT (2022), GPT-4 (2023), GPT-4o (2024), and so on. The architecture uses masked self-attention so each token attends only to earlier tokens, making it naturally suited to text generation. Nearly all modern open-source LLMs — Llama, Mistral, Qwen — share this decoder-only backbone.
why_it_matters: GPT set the template for modern LLMs. Understanding 'decoder-only + causal mask + next-token prediction' explains 90% of architectural choices you will see in the wild.

title: Llama (Model Family)
short_definition: Meta's open-weights LLM family — Llama, Llama 2, Llama 3, Llama 4 — that defined the open-source frontier since 2023.
full_explanation: Llama (2023) was the first open-weights model to match GPT-3.5 on many tasks. Llama 2 (mid-2023) introduced a commercial-friendly license. Llama 3 (2024) came in 8B, 70B, and 405B sizes, with 3.1/3.2/3.3 incrementally extending context, multimodality, and edge variants. Llama 4 (2025) shifted to mixture-of-experts with Scout, Maverick, and Behemoth variants. The Llama ecosystem — fine-tunes, quantizations, community tooling — is the default substrate for open LLM work.
why_it_matters: Llama is the single largest tide that lifted the open-source AI boat. Most open models in production are Llama or Llama-derived.

title: Mistral (Model Family)
short_definition: Mistral AI's open-weights models — Mistral 7B, Mixtral 8x7B, Mixtral 8x22B, Mistral Large, Ministral — known for efficiency and MoE pioneering.
full_explanation: Mistral 7B (late 2023) was the first sub-10B model to challenge Llama 2 13B, introducing sliding-window attention and grouped-query attention. Mixtral 8x7B (early 2024) was the first major open MoE model. Mistral Large and subsequent closed models compete with GPT-4-class systems. Ministral variants target edge and on-device deployment. The company open-sourced heavily under Apache 2.0 early on, with newer models shifting to research or commercial licensing.
why_it_matters: Mistral pushed the efficiency frontier. Many architectural choices (sliding window, MoE at open-weights scale) began in their models.

title: Mixtral
short_definition: Mistral's mixture-of-experts models — 8×7B and 8×22B — with total parameters of 46B and 141B but only ~13B and ~39B active per token.
full_explanation: Mixtral 8×7B (Dec 2023) popularized open MoE. Each token routes to 2 of 8 experts at each layer; 'total' parameters live in VRAM but only active ones run per forward pass. Quality approaches Llama 2 70B at roughly the inference cost of a 13B dense model. Mixtral 8×22B (Apr 2024) scaled the recipe. Both are Apache 2.0 and ship in most inference backends.
why_it_matters: Mixtral proved MoE at open-weights scale was viable and kicked off the MoE wave that includes DeepSeek-V3 and Llama 4.

title: Qwen (Model Family)
short_definition: Alibaba's open-weights LLM family — Qwen, Qwen2, Qwen2.5, Qwen3 — strong on multilingual tasks, code, math, and agentic capabilities.
full_explanation: The Qwen family (Alibaba/Qwen team) releases dense and MoE variants from under 1B to over 70B, plus vision-language, audio, and math specialist models. Qwen2.5 (late 2024) was competitive with GPT-4o mini and Llama 3.1 70B on many benchmarks at smaller sizes. Multilingual coverage is especially strong in Chinese and many Asian languages. License is permissive on most weights.
why_it_matters: Qwen is one of the top-tier open-weights families. For multilingual or Asian-language deployments, it is often the best choice.

title: Phi (Small Language Model Family)
short_definition: Microsoft's small language models — Phi-2, Phi-3, Phi-4 — demonstrating that carefully curated 'textbook-quality' data can make sub-10B models competitive with much larger ones.
full_explanation: Phi-2 (2023) showed 2.7B could rival 13B models on reasoning via curated data. Phi-3 (2024) shipped at 3.8B, 7B, and 14B with strong multitask performance. Phi-4 (2024–2025) prioritized reasoning with synthetic data built by a frontier teacher. The Phi thesis: data quality trumps raw token count for small models. Widely used for on-device and latency-sensitive applications.
why_it_matters: Phi reshaped the small-model conversation. If your target is under 14B with chat-quality reasoning, Phi is a top contender.
common_misconceptions: Phi's benchmarks often exceed its general helpfulness — the curated data tilt can leave gaps on casual chitchat.

title: Gemma (Model Family)
short_definition: Google's open-weights models (Gemma 1, 2, 3) derived from the same research as Gemini, sized from 1B to 27B with strong quality-per-parameter.
full_explanation: Gemma is Google's open-weights counterpart to the closed Gemini family. Gemma 2 (mid-2024) introduced interleaved local and global attention for efficient long-context, strong 9B and 27B variants. Gemma 3 adds multimodality and improved instruction tuning. Licensing is permissive but includes a Responsible AI use clause. Gemma is one of the more polished open families when you want Google-lineage behavior without a closed API.
why_it_matters: Gemma is a top-tier option in the 2–27B range. It often wins on per-parameter quality for deployments that value efficiency.

title: Gemini (Model Family)
short_definition: Google DeepMind's closed multimodal LLM family — Gemini Pro, Ultra, Flash, Nano — with strong long-context and image/video/audio understanding.
full_explanation: Gemini (2023) launched as Google's GPT-4 competitor with native multimodality. Gemini 1.5 Pro (2024) brought a 1M-token context window. Gemini 2 series continued with stronger reasoning, on-device Nano variants, and integrated tooling. Available through Google AI Studio, Vertex AI, and the Google Cloud ecosystem. Known for long-context performance and video understanding ahead of most competitors.
why_it_matters: Gemini is one of three frontier closed-model families. Its long-context strength is unmatched in some workloads.

title: Claude (Model Family)
short_definition: Anthropic's closed LLM family — Haiku, Sonnet, Opus variants across Claude 2, 3, 3.5, and 4 — known for strong writing quality, safety training, and agentic capability.
full_explanation: Claude debuted in 2023, with Claude 2 introducing a 100k-token context and Claude 3 (Mar 2024) restructuring into Haiku (fast/small), Sonnet (balanced), and Opus (strongest). Claude 3.5 Sonnet (mid-2024) hit frontier-class coding and reasoning. Claude 4 extended context and agentic reliability. Trained with Constitutional AI and extensive RLHF/RLAIF. Claude powers Claude.ai, the Anthropic API, Amazon Bedrock, and Google Vertex.
why_it_matters: Claude is a top-three frontier model family. It is particularly popular for coding, long-form writing, and agentic workflows.

title: DeepSeek (Model Family)
short_definition: A Chinese lab's open-weights MoE and dense models — DeepSeek V2, V3, R1 — notable for strong reasoning and very low training cost.
full_explanation: DeepSeek released DeepSeek V2 (mid-2024) and V3 (late 2024), both large MoE models with very strong quality-per-dollar. DeepSeek-R1 (early 2025) was the first major open reasoning model, trained with RL on a process-reward-style objective, matching OpenAI o1 on many benchmarks and released with a permissive license. The distilled R1-Distill variants brought reasoning to small Qwen and Llama students.
why_it_matters: DeepSeek broke the frontier-reasoning paywall. R1 and its distilled variants made strong reasoning models free and local.

title: DeepSeek R1
short_definition: DeepSeek's open-weights reasoning model that achieved OpenAI o1-level performance using large-scale RL on a MoE base, released early 2025.
full_explanation: DeepSeek-R1 (Jan 2025) is a reasoning-tuned variant of DeepSeek-V3 (671B total, 37B active). It was trained with multi-stage RL that rewarded correctness on verifiable tasks (math, code), letting the model learn long chain-of-thought with explicit reflection tokens. R1-Distill variants (Qwen-1.5B through 32B, Llama-8B, Llama-70B) transfer R1's reasoning into smaller dense students via SFT on R1's traces. Permissive license, MIT-compatible in most uses.
why_it_matters: R1 is the open-weights reference point for reasoning models. Its release collapsed the closed-vs-open reasoning gap overnight.

title: OpenAI o1 / o3 (Reasoning Models)
short_definition: OpenAI's first public reasoning models (o1 series, late 2024; o3 series, 2025) that trade extra inference-time 'thinking' for sharply better performance on hard tasks.
full_explanation: o1 (Sept 2024) was trained with large-scale RL to produce long internal chains of thought before answering — the hidden 'thinking' tokens are not shown to users but dramatically lift performance on competition math, code, and science. o3 (2025) extended the approach with agentic tool use and higher compute budgets, showing strong gains on ARC-AGI and graduate-level benchmarks. Inference is notably more expensive — a single answer can spend millions of tokens of thinking.
why_it_matters: o1/o3 prove that test-time compute is a new scaling axis. Other labs (Claude, DeepSeek-R1) have since shipped similar models.

title: Whisper (Speech Recognition)
short_definition: OpenAI's open-weight speech-to-text model family, trained on 680k hours of multilingual audio, with strong robustness to noise and accents.
full_explanation: Whisper (Sept 2022) is an encoder-decoder transformer trained on a massive weakly supervised audio-text corpus. It transcribes 99 languages, translates speech to English, and performs voice-activity detection and timestamp prediction. Sizes range from tiny (39M) to large-v3 (1.5B). Open-weights and MIT-licensed. CTranslate2 and faster-whisper implementations enable real-time transcription on modest hardware.
why_it_matters: Whisper is the de facto open speech-recognition model. Most meeting-transcription tools and voice-agent pipelines rely on it.

title: CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining)
short_definition: OpenAI's model that embeds images and text into a shared space via contrastive learning on 400M image-caption pairs — the backbone of most modern VLMs and image search.
full_explanation: CLIP (Radford et al. 2021) trains an image encoder and a text encoder jointly so that matching (image, caption) pairs have high cosine similarity in a shared embedding space. The learned space supports zero-shot classification ('Is this a cat? Score cat vs. dog text embeddings against the image'), text-to-image search, and serves as the vision tower of LLaVA-style VLMs. SigLIP and OpenCLIP extend and improve the recipe.
why_it_matters: CLIP is the most influential vision model of the last decade. Every modern multimodal system stands on its shoulders.

title: SigLIP
short_definition: Google's CLIP successor that replaces the softmax contrastive loss with a sigmoid loss, improving scaling and quality especially for smaller batches.
full_explanation: SigLIP (Zhai et al. 2023) swaps CLIP's softmax across the batch for a per-pair sigmoid, removing the dependency on very large batch sizes for contrastive learning. The resulting embeddings are stronger at zero-shot classification and retrieval, and SigLIP is now the default vision tower for many VLMs including Gemma and LLaVA-Next.
why_it_matters: SigLIP replaced CLIP as the preferred vision encoder for modern VLMs. Its design choices spread quickly.

title: Stable Diffusion
short_definition: Stability AI's open-weights latent-diffusion text-to-image model family (SD 1.5, SDXL, SD3) that democratized generative imagery.
full_explanation: Stable Diffusion (Aug 2022) operates in a compressed latent space produced by a VAE, letting a diffusion UNet denoise at a fraction of the cost of pixel-space diffusion. SDXL (mid-2023) scaled up with a two-stage refiner; SD3 (2024) moved to a transformer-based (DiT) backbone with rectified flow. The permissive initial license triggered the open image-generation ecosystem — LoRA artists, ControlNet, img2img tooling, and countless fine-tunes.
why_it_matters: Stable Diffusion is the open counterpart to DALL·E and Midjourney. It is the foundation of nearly all local image-generation workflows.

title: Diffusion Model
short_definition: A generative modeling approach that learns to reverse a gradual noising process, producing samples from pure noise in a series of denoising steps.
full_explanation: Diffusion models (Ho et al. 2020, Song et al. 2021) train a neural network to predict the noise added to data at each timestep of a forward noising chain. At inference, you sample Gaussian noise and iteratively denoise it back to a clean sample. Variants: score-based, DDPM, DDIM (faster sampling), and rectified-flow or flow-matching (even faster). Dominant for image (Stable Diffusion), video (Sora, Veo), audio, and molecular generation.
why_it_matters: Diffusion is the dominant generative framework outside language. Understanding it is essential for anyone working on image, video, or audio generation.

title: LLaVA
short_definition: An open-source vision-language model family that glues a CLIP/SigLIP vision encoder onto an LLM via a lightweight projection layer, with instruction tuning on image-text pairs.
full_explanation: LLaVA (Liu et al. 2023) introduced a simple and effective recipe for building VLMs: use a frozen vision encoder (originally CLIP) to extract image features, project them into the LLM's token embedding space with a two-layer MLP, and instruction-tune on synthetic (image, question, answer) triples. LLaVA-Next and LLaVA-OneVision extend it with higher resolution, SigLIP encoders, and multi-image support. Widely used as the reference open VLM architecture.
why_it_matters: LLaVA's recipe is what most open VLMs still follow. For custom VLM projects, it is the canonical starting point.

title: BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)
short_definition: Google's 2018 encoder-only transformer trained with masked language modeling — the foundation of most pre-2023 NLP pipelines.
full_explanation: BERT (Devlin et al. 2018) was the first widely deployed large pretrained encoder. Trained with masked-language modeling and next-sentence prediction, it produced contextualized token embeddings that could be fine-tuned for classification, NER, and QA with a small task head. BERT is bidirectional (each token sees the whole sequence) — contrasted with GPT's causal masking. Descendants: RoBERTa, DeBERTa, ALBERT, ELECTRA. Mostly superseded for generation tasks by decoder-only LLMs, but still the go-to for classification and retrieval.
why_it_matters: BERT is alive and well for retrieval (dense encoders) and classification. Its architectural lineage powers most embedding models.

title: RoBERTa
short_definition: A robustly optimized BERT — same architecture, different pretraining recipe — that dropped NSP, trained longer on more data, and consistently outperforms BERT on classification tasks.
full_explanation: RoBERTa (Liu et al. 2019) kept BERT's architecture but showed that with more data, bigger batches, longer training, and removal of the next-sentence-prediction objective, the same model hits materially higher scores on GLUE and similar benchmarks. It is a canonical example of how pretraining recipes matter as much as architecture.
why_it_matters: For NLP classification and NER baselines, RoBERTa is usually a stronger starting point than BERT.

title: T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer)
short_definition: Google's encoder-decoder model that frames every NLP task — classification, translation, summarization — as text-to-text generation.
full_explanation: T5 (Raffel et al. 2020) unified NLP tasks under one format: input text in, output text out. Trained on a 'span corruption' objective (mask spans, predict the missing tokens). Comes in sizes from 60M to 11B. FLAN-T5 (2022) is instruction-tuned T5 and remains a strong small seq2seq baseline. For classification and structured output, encoder-decoder models like T5 can be more efficient than decoder-only LLMs at small scales.
why_it_matters: T5 popularized the text-to-text paradigm. For constrained-generation tasks at small scale, it often beats decoder-only alternatives.

title: BART
short_definition: Facebook AI's encoder-decoder transformer pretrained as a denoising autoencoder, strong on summarization and other seq2seq tasks.
full_explanation: BART (Lewis et al. 2019) combines a BERT-like bidirectional encoder with a GPT-like autoregressive decoder.
