Pretraining corrupts text (token masking, sentence permutation, document rotation) and trains the model to reconstruct it. BART-large became the default summarization baseline for years, and its bilingual variant mBART supports translation. Still competitive at small scale for tasks where encoder-decoder shines.
why_it_matters: BART is a historical workhorse for summarization and structured seq2seq. Still a reasonable small-model option today.

title: StarCoder (Code Model)
short_definition: BigCode's open-source code LLM family trained on permissively licensed GitHub data, with strong code completion and repo-level context.
full_explanation: StarCoder (2023) and StarCoder 2 (2024) are Apache-2.0 code LLMs trained on The Stack, a corpus of permissively licensed GitHub repos. Sizes range from 3B to 15B. Strong at Python, JavaScript, and major languages; supports fill-in-the-middle (FIM) for code completion. StarCoder 2 added better multilingual code coverage. Widely used for open-source code-completion assistants and inference on local GPUs.
why_it_matters: StarCoder is the most permissively licensed serious code model. Many open Copilot-alternatives build on it.

title: CodeLlama
short_definition: Meta's code specialization of Llama 2, fine-tuned on 500B+ code tokens with long-context and infilling support.
full_explanation: Code Llama (Aug 2023) started from Llama 2 weights and continued pretraining on code, followed by instruction tuning in the Python-specialized and general Code Llama Instruct variants. Sizes: 7B, 13B, 34B, 70B. Supports fill-in-the-middle for completion. Llama 3.1 405B largely supersedes Code Llama at frontier scale, but CodeLlama remains popular for self-hosted completion on consumer hardware.
why_it_matters: Code Llama was the first widely deployed open-source code model in the 70B class. Still a strong self-host option below 13B.

title: Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM)
short_definition: A code-model training objective that teaches the model to predict text between a prefix and suffix, enabling true code-completion inside existing files.
full_explanation: FIM (Bavarian et al. 2022) trains a causal LM to handle spans of missing text. During training, some documents are randomly split into [prefix, middle, suffix] and rearranged as [prefix, <suffix_token>, suffix, <middle_token>, middle]. At inference, tools prompt with prefix + suffix + special tokens, and the model generates the middle. Every serious code model (CodeLlama, StarCoder, DeepSeek-Coder, Qwen-Coder) supports FIM.
why_it_matters: FIM is why IDE code completion works inside functions, not just at the end. It is a prerequisite for Copilot-style UX.

title: DeepSeek Coder
short_definition: DeepSeek's code-specialist LLM family with strong completion, infilling, and long-context performance on programming benchmarks.
full_explanation: DeepSeek-Coder (2023) and DeepSeek-Coder-V2 (2024) focus on code tasks with extensive pretraining on a large code corpus (87 languages) and repository-level context. Sizes range from 1.3B to 33B dense and up to a 236B MoE variant. V2 extended context to 128k tokens. On HumanEval, MBPP, and SWE-Bench the series is competitive with proprietary code models and open-weights under a permissive license.
why_it_matters: DeepSeek-Coder is a top open-weights option for code completion and agentic coding on self-hosted infra.

title: Qwen Coder
short_definition: Alibaba's code-specialist variant of Qwen, strong on repo-level context, agentic code tasks, and multilingual programming.
full_explanation: Qwen2.5-Coder (2024) and successors are trained with heavy code-centric post-training. Sizes span from 0.5B to 32B. They lead many open-source code benchmarks while retaining general instruction-following. Particularly strong at Chinese-language code documentation and reasoning-in-code tasks. Apache-2.0 licensed for most variants.
why_it_matters: Qwen-Coder is one of the top code models in the open-weights space alongside DeepSeek-Coder.

title: Falcon (Model Family)
short_definition: TII's (UAE) open-weights LLM family — Falcon 7B/40B/180B, then Falcon 2 and Falcon 3 — one of the early strong open alternatives to Llama.
full_explanation: Falcon (2023) was trained on the RefinedWeb corpus — a high-quality Common Crawl subset — and briefly held the top Open LLM Leaderboard spot. Falcon 180B (Sept 2023) was the largest openly released model at the time. Subsequent Falcon 2 and Falcon 3 added multimodality and efficiency improvements. Released under a modified license with permissive commercial use.
why_it_matters: Falcon pushed open-source to near-frontier scale before Llama 2, and its RefinedWeb dataset influenced later curation efforts.

title: Command R (Cohere)
short_definition: Cohere's open-weights LLM family (Command R and R+), tuned for enterprise use with strong RAG, tool use, and multilingual support.
full_explanation: Command R (35B, Mar 2024) and Command R+ (104B) target enterprise RAG and tool-calling. Trained with an emphasis on citation generation, structured output, and 10 major business languages. Open-weights under CC-BY-NC for research; commercial via the Cohere API. Popular with enterprise teams wanting on-prem-deployable RAG assistants.
why_it_matters: Command R is one of the few open models purpose-built for RAG with citations. Strong default for enterprise assistant projects.

title: Granite (IBM Model Family)
short_definition: IBM's open-weights LLMs designed for enterprise governance — known training data, clear provenance, and Apache 2.0 licensing.
full_explanation: Granite (2023) and Granite 3 (2024) are IBM's open models for enterprise. The emphasis is governance: fully documented training data (with provenance reporting), explicit IP indemnification via watsonx, and permissive licensing. Sizes span from 1B for edge to 34B code-specialized variants. Common pick in regulated industries where 'what was this trained on?' matters.
why_it_matters: Granite fills a niche that open alternatives often neglect: full provenance and enterprise indemnification.

title: SAM (Segment Anything Model)
short_definition: Meta's foundation model for promptable image segmentation — given a point, box, or mask, it produces a precise object mask zero-shot.
full_explanation: SAM (Apr 2023) was trained on 1.1B masks over 11M images, enabling zero-shot segmentation from a variety of prompts (points, boxes, free-form masks). SAM 2 (Jul 2024) extended to video with memory across frames. Widely used as a primitive in image-editing pipelines, medical imaging, and robotics. Apache-2.0 licensed.
why_it_matters: SAM made segmentation zero-shot for practical use cases. It is the 'vision primitive' most modern image-editing tools depend on.

title: DINOv2 (Self-Supervised Vision)
short_definition: Meta's self-supervised vision transformer that produces strong image features without any labels — the state-of-the-art self-supervised vision backbone.
full_explanation: DINOv2 (2023) trains a ViT via self-distillation on 142M curated images. The features transfer to classification, segmentation, depth, and retrieval with only a small task head — often beating supervised pretraining. Widely used where CLIP is too text-biased or where labels are not available. Sizes: ViT-S/14, B/14, L/14, g/14.
why_it_matters: For non-text visual tasks, DINOv2 features often outperform CLIP and are a safer self-supervised backbone.

title: Vision Transformer (ViT)
short_definition: A transformer architecture that treats image patches as tokens, replacing CNNs as the dominant backbone for large-scale vision models.
full_explanation: ViT (Dosovitskiy et al. 2020) splits an image into fixed-size patches, linearly embeds each patch, adds positional embeddings, and feeds the sequence into a standard transformer encoder. At sufficient data scale (JFT-300M+), ViTs match or beat CNNs. They underpin nearly every modern vision encoder — CLIP, SigLIP, DINOv2, SAM, and most VLM vision towers.
why_it_matters: ViT is the vision architecture of the transformer era. Understanding it explains why CLIP, VLMs, and modern vision foundation models work.

title: YOLO (You Only Look Once)
short_definition: A family of real-time object detection models — YOLOv1 through YOLO11 — that perform detection in a single forward pass for high FPS inference.
full_explanation: YOLO (Redmon et al. 2016) frames object detection as direct regression of bounding boxes and class probabilities from full images in one pass, trading some accuracy for speed. The family is now maintained by multiple groups: Ultralytics (YOLOv8, YOLO11), YOLOv7 authors, and YOLO-World for open-vocabulary detection. Real-time performance on edge devices and clear deployment paths make YOLO the default for production detection.
why_it_matters: For real-time detection on edge, YOLO is nearly always the right starting point. Simple to fine-tune and widely supported.

title: DETR (Detection Transformer)
short_definition: An end-to-end object detector that replaces hand-crafted anchor boxes and NMS with a transformer decoder and bipartite matching.
full_explanation: DETR (Carion et al. 2020) formulates detection as a set-prediction problem: a transformer decoder produces a fixed set of N box predictions in parallel, and training matches them to ground truth via Hungarian algorithm. No anchors, no NMS. Successors (Deformable DETR, DINO-DETR, RT-DETR) fix the original's slow convergence and make DETR competitive with YOLO on both accuracy and latency.
why_it_matters: DETR introduced the transformer paradigm to detection. Its successors are now on par with YOLO for many tasks.

title: Sora (Video Generation)
short_definition: OpenAI's text-to-video diffusion-transformer model (announced Feb 2024, public 2024), generating coherent multi-second HD video from text prompts.
full_explanation: Sora is a diffusion transformer operating on spacetime patches of video latent representations. It can generate up to minute-long clips with consistent subjects and camera movement. Training uses a mix of video and image data at variable resolution and duration, giving it flexibility across aspect ratios and lengths. Closed-access via OpenAI, with competitors like Runway Gen-3, Kling, Veo 2, and Pika iterating aggressively.
why_it_matters: Sora set the benchmark for generative video. Other models now chase its quality bar.

title: Veo (Google Video Model)
short_definition: Google DeepMind's text-to-video generation model (Veo 1, Veo 2, Veo 3) — strong on cinematic quality, motion, and prompt adherence.
full_explanation: Veo (May 2024) and Veo 2 (late 2024) generate HD video from text or image prompts. Veo 3 (2025) added native audio generation (speech and ambient) synchronized to video. Available via Google VideoFX, Vertex AI, and Workspace integrations. Competes directly with Sora, Runway Gen-3, and Kling in the commercial video-generation space.
why_it_matters: Veo is one of the leading closed video models. Veo 3's synchronized audio is a notable capability jump.

title: Flux (Black Forest Labs)
short_definition: Black Forest Labs' open-weights text-to-image diffusion transformer family — FLUX.1 [dev], [pro], [schnell] — pushing open image quality past SDXL.
full_explanation: FLUX.1 (2024), built by former Stability AI researchers, is a flow-matching diffusion transformer with 12B parameters. Variants: [pro] (closed API), [dev] (non-commercial weights), [schnell] (fast, permissive). FLUX.1 quality significantly exceeds SDXL and rivals Midjourney on prompt adherence and detail. The image-generation open-weights frontier moved from Stable Diffusion to FLUX during 2024–2025.
why_it_matters: FLUX is the current open-weights image-generation frontier. Most serious open image pipelines now use it.

title: Mamba (State Space Model)
short_definition: An LLM architecture based on selective state-space models instead of attention, with linear-time inference and strong long-context performance.
full_explanation: Mamba (Gu & Dao 2023) replaces transformer self-attention with a selective state-space layer (S6). Inference is O(N) in sequence length rather than O(N²), with a fixed memory footprint per token regardless of context size. Language-modeling perplexity is competitive with similar-sized transformers on shorter sequences, with significant advantages on very long contexts. Hybrid architectures (Jamba, Mamba-2, Zamba) mix Mamba layers with attention for the best of both.
why_it_matters: Mamba is the most viable non-transformer LLM architecture. Hybrid SSM-attention models are a real alternative going forward.

title: State Space Model
short_definition: A sequence-model class that processes tokens through a linear recurrence with a learned hidden state, enabling linear-time inference.
full_explanation: State space models (SSMs) treat sequences like continuous-time dynamical systems: a hidden state h_t evolves via h_t = A h_{t-1} + B x_t and outputs y_t = C h_t + D x_t. S4 (Gu et al. 2021) made them practical on discrete sequences; Mamba introduced input-dependent (selective) transitions. SSMs scale linearly in sequence length during both training and inference, offering substantial long-context advantages over quadratic attention.
why_it_matters: SSMs are the most promising alternative to attention for long-context workloads. Understanding them matters for the next architecture wave.

title: RWKV
short_definition: A linear-attention RNN-transformer hybrid that trains like a transformer but runs inference like an RNN, with linear-time generation.
full_explanation: RWKV (Peng et al. 2023) reformulates attention as a linear recurrence that can be unrolled in parallel for training (transformer mode) and computed recurrently at inference (RNN mode) — combining training efficiency with O(1) memory per generated token. Versions through RWKV-7 scaled to tens of billions of parameters with quality approaching similarly sized transformers. Community-driven, fully open.
why_it_matters: RWKV is a serious open alternative for edge and long-context deployment where transformer KV cache is prohibitive.

title: Jamba (Hybrid Mamba-Transformer)
short_definition: AI21 Labs' hybrid architecture interleaving Mamba and transformer attention layers, supporting 256k+ context with strong throughput.
full_explanation: Jamba (Mar 2024) interleaves Mamba, transformer attention, and MoE layers in a specific ratio. The hybrid captures Mamba's long-context efficiency and attention's information-routing strengths. Jamba-1.5 supports 256k tokens with effective long-context retention. Open-weights for research and permissive API access.
why_it_matters: Jamba is the first production-deployed hybrid SSM-attention LLM. It demonstrates the approach scales.

title: Encoder
short_definition: A model (or model half) that transforms input tokens into contextualized representations — the bidirectional half of encoder-decoder and the whole of BERT-style models.
full_explanation: Encoders read the entire input and produce per-token contextualized vectors. They use bidirectional self-attention (no causal mask), letting every position see every other. Useful for classification, NER, and retrieval. Encoder-decoder models (T5, BART) pair an encoder with an autoregressive decoder. Decoder-only models (GPT, Llama) have no encoder and treat input and output as one sequence with causal attention.
why_it_matters: Understanding encoder vs. decoder attention is prerequisite to reasoning about which model fits which task.

title: Decoder
short_definition: A model (or half) that generates tokens autoregressively, using causal attention so each token sees only previous tokens — the whole of GPT-family models.
full_explanation: Decoders generate text one token at a time. Causal self-attention masks future positions during training, enabling teacher-forced training where each position predicts its next while seeing only its past. At inference, the decoder generates token by token, appending each to the context. Every modern LLM — Llama, Qwen, Mistral, Claude, Gemini — uses decoder-only architecture.
why_it_matters: Decoder-only is the dominant modern LLM architecture. Every design decision about LLMs traces back to this choice.

title: Encoder-Decoder
short_definition: An architecture with a bidirectional encoder that reads the input and a causal decoder that generates the output, bridged by cross-attention.
full_explanation: Encoder-decoder models (T5, BART, MarianMT, NLLB) were the standard for seq2seq tasks (translation, summarization) pre-LLM era. The encoder produces a contextual representation of the input; the decoder attends to it via cross-attention while generating. At small scales, encoder-decoders can outperform decoder-only LLMs on structured seq2seq, but decoder-only LLMs generally dominate at scale.
why_it_matters: For some tasks (translation, structured extraction at small scale), encoder-decoder still wins. Know the trade-off.

title: Base Model
short_definition: A pretrained LLM that has not undergone instruction tuning or alignment — it completes text but does not follow chat-style instructions.
full_explanation: A base model is the direct output of pretraining on next-token prediction. Give it 'Once upon a time' and it will continue the story; give it 'What is the capital of France?' and it might continue with more questions rather than answering. Base models are useful for further training (SFT, domain continued pretraining, DPO). They are rarely deployed directly to users. Llama-3-8B is a base model; Llama-3-8B-Instruct is its chat-tuned descendant.
why_it_matters: Starting from a strong base model determines your ceiling. Base model choice matters more than any post-training trick.

title: Chat Model (Instruct Model)
short_definition: A base model that has been post-trained (SFT + preference optimization) to follow instructions and hold conversations.
full_explanation: Chat or 'Instruct' models are the user-facing variant. They understand chat templates, follow instructions, maintain persona, and refuse harmful requests. Examples: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct. Chat models are what you plug into assistants, agents, and RAG pipelines — base models alone will not do.
why_it_matters: When you see 'Instruct' or 'Chat' in a model name, that is what you want for applications. Base variants are for further training.

title: Embedding Model
short_definition: A model that maps text, images, or other inputs into fixed-size dense vectors where semantic similarity corresponds to vector proximity.
full_explanation: Embedding models — text-embedding-3, Cohere Embed v3, BGE-M3, Nomic-Embed, Voyage — are trained with contrastive objectives so similar inputs land near each other in vector space. Outputs are typically 384–4096 dimensional vectors. Used for retrieval, clustering, classification, and recommendations. Matryoshka representation learning lets one model emit vectors that work at multiple truncated dimensions.
why_it_matters: Every RAG pipeline, semantic search system, and recommender depends on embedding-model quality.

title: BGE Embeddings
short_definition: BAAI's BGE (BAAI General Embedding) family — strong open-weights text embedding models, now including multilingual and multi-functional variants.
full_explanation: BGE (2023) and BGE-M3 (2024) are BAAI's open embedding models. BGE-M3 combines dense, sparse, and multi-vector (ColBERT-style) representations in a single model, and supports 100+ languages. BGE is the default open-weights pick for retrieval on the MTEB leaderboard at its size class and is often used as the starting point for domain-specific embedding fine-tunes.
why_it_matters: BGE is the go-to open embedding model for RAG. BGE-M3 in particular covers most deployment needs.

title: Nomic Embed
short_definition: Nomic AI's open-weights text embedding models with fully reproducible training (open data + open code) and strong MTEB performance.
full_explanation: Nomic-Embed-Text-v1 (Feb 2024) was the first embedding model released with open weights, open data, and open training code reproducing MTEB-competitive results. Later versions (v1.5, v2) add Matryoshka truncation and multilingual support. Widely used for RAG by teams that want audit-ready embeddings. Also known for the Nomic Atlas visualization tool.
why_it_matters: Nomic Embed is the choice for teams that need full provenance — open data, open weights, audit trail.

title: Retrieval (IR)
short_definition: The process of finding relevant documents or passages from a large corpus in response to a query — the first 'R' in RAG.
full_explanation: Retrieval is classical information retrieval brought into ML pipelines. Given a query, a retriever scores corpus items and returns the top-k. Types: sparse (BM25, TF-IDF), dense (embedding-based), and hybrid. Evaluation metrics: recall@k, MRR, nDCG. Retrieval quality is the ceiling on RAG quality — a great generator cannot save a bad retriever. 'Garbage in, garbage out' applies aggressively.
why_it_matters: Most RAG failures trace to retrieval, not generation. Getting retrieval right is the single highest-leverage RAG investment.

title: Dense Retrieval
short_definition: Retrieval based on embedding queries and documents into a shared vector space and using cosine similarity (or dot product) for matching.
full_explanation: Dense retrieval trains or uses an embedding model that projects queries and documents close in vector space when semantically related. Approximate nearest neighbor indexes (HNSW, IVF-PQ) make it practical at billion-document scale. Strengths: semantic matching, paraphrase handling. Weaknesses: keyword precision, named entities, rare terms. Usually combined with sparse retrieval in hybrid setups.
why_it_matters: Dense retrieval is the default modern RAG retriever. It handles semantic queries where BM25 fails.
common_misconceptions: Dense retrieval is not automatically better than BM25. For exact-match and rare-term queries, sparse often wins.

title: Sparse Retrieval
short_definition: Retrieval based on sparse lexical representations — BM25, TF-IDF, or learned sparse models like SPLADE — matching on token overlap.
full_explanation: Sparse retrievers represent queries and documents as sparse vectors over vocabulary tokens (or learned sub-terms). BM25 is the canonical lexical method and remains competitive decades later. Learned sparse retrievers (SPLADE, DRAGON-S) use LLMs to expand and weight terms, capturing some semantic matching while staying interpretable. Sparse retrieval is unmatched for exact-match, rare-term, and code-heavy queries.
why_it_matters: Any serious RAG stack includes sparse retrieval alongside dense. Keyword precision is worth keeping.

title: BM25
short_definition: A classic sparse retrieval scoring function based on term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length normalization.
full_explanation: BM25 (Okapi BM25, Robertson 1994) scores each document against a query as a sum over query terms of TF·IDF with saturation and length normalization. Parameters k1 (typically 1.2–2) and b (typically 0.75) tune sensitivity to term frequency and document length. Forty years old and still competitive — many modern systems use BM25 as one leg of hybrid retrieval, often with LLM-reranking on top.
why_it_matters: BM25 is the best-tested retrieval primitive. Most modern RAG stacks use it alongside embeddings.

title: Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search
short_definition: Algorithms that find the k nearest vectors in high-dimensional space much faster than exact search, by trading a small recall loss for large speedups.
full_explanation: Exact nearest-neighbor search is O(N·d) per query — impractical for millions of vectors. ANN algorithms (HNSW, IVF, IVF-PQ, ScaNN, Annoy, FAISS flat variants) cluster or graph-structure the index to prune the search space, returning approximate top-k in sublinear time. Typical trade-offs: recall@10 of 95–99% for 10–100× speedups. The precise algorithm and parameters depend on dataset size, query rate, and quality requirements.
why_it_matters: ANN is the engine that makes vector databases practical. Without it, dense retrieval would be unusable at scale.

title: Product Quantization (PQ)
short_definition: A vector-compression technique that splits each vector into sub-vectors and quantizes each independently, shrinking index size by 4–32×.
full_explanation: Product Quantization (Jégou et al. 2011) divides a D-dimensional vector into M sub-vectors, runs k-means on each sub-vector dimension across the corpus to produce codebooks, and stores each vector as M byte codes (1 byte per sub-vector, indexing into 256 centroids). Search uses a lookup table of distances between query sub-vectors and each codebook, approximating full-vector distance. Paired with IVF (IVF-PQ) for billion-scale search.
why_it_matters: PQ is how vector databases fit billion-vector corpora in reasonable memory. The memory savings are often 10–20×.

title: IVF (Inverted File Index)
short_definition: A vector-index structure that partitions vectors into clusters via k-means; queries search only the nearest clusters instead of the full corpus.
full_explanation: IVF runs k-means on corpus vectors to produce nlist centroids. Each vector is assigned to its nearest centroid. At query time, find the nprobe nearest centroids to the query and search only vectors in those clusters. nprobe is the quality/speed knob — higher gives better recall, lower gives faster search. IVF alone is fast but memory-heavy; IVF-PQ adds compression.
why_it_matters: IVF is a building block in FAISS and most vector databases. Knowing nprobe tuning is essential for latency-sensitive deployments.

title: FAISS
short_definition: Meta's open-source library for efficient similarity search and clustering of dense vectors, the de facto reference for ANN algorithms.
full_explanation: FAISS (Johnson et al. 2017) implements IVF, IVF-PQ, HNSW, LSH, and many more index types, with optimized CPU and GPU kernels. It is the engine inside many vector databases (Milvus, Qdrant optionally, LanceDB) and the reference library for research on ANN. Usable standalone via Python bindings for applications that need full control over index trade-offs.
why_it_matters: FAISS is the canonical ANN library. Understanding its index types is the foundation for choosing any vector database.

title: ColBERT (Late Interaction Retrieval)
short_definition: A retrieval model that encodes each document token separately and scores a query by summing the maximum similarity per query token — late interaction.
full_explanation: ColBERT (Khattab & Zaharia 2020) sits between bi-encoders (one vector per doc) and cross-encoders (joint encoding, expensive). It encodes each token of both query and document into contextualized vectors and scores via MaxSim: for each query token, find its best matching document token, sum the similarities. Quality approaches cross-encoders at 100× less inference cost. ColBERTv2 and PLAID add compression and efficient search.
why_it_matters: ColBERT is a strong compromise between retrieval speed and reranking-quality precision.

title: Cross-Encoder
short_definition: A model that takes a query and document together as a single input and outputs a relevance score — high accuracy, expensive to use at scale.
full_explanation: A cross-encoder concatenates (query, document) and feeds the pair into a single transformer with a classification or regression head. Because query and document tokens attend to each other directly, scoring is highly accurate. But every candidate needs a full forward pass — O(k·doc_len) at query time — so cross-encoders are used only for reranking a small candidate set (10–100 docs) after cheaper retrieval.
why_it_matters: Cross-encoders are the gold standard for reranking. Stacking them on top of dense retrieval is a standard RAG pattern.

title: Bi-Encoder
short_definition: A retrieval architecture that encodes queries and documents independently into fixed-size vectors, allowing fast similarity lookup.
full_explanation: Bi-encoders (also called two-tower or Siamese encoders) run query and document through the same or twin transformer, producing one vector each. Relevance is cosine similarity or dot product. Fast because document vectors can be precomputed and indexed. Trade-off: no cross-attention between query and doc, so relevance modeling is weaker than cross-encoders. Most embedding-based retrieval models (BGE, E5, Nomic-Embed) are bi-encoders.
why_it_matters: Bi-encoders power nearly all production dense retrieval. Understanding the bi-encoder vs. cross-encoder trade-off is essential.

title: SPLADE (Sparse Learned Retrieval)
short_definition: A retrieval model that produces sparse term-weighted representations via BERT, combining lexical interpretability with learned semantic expansion.
full_explanation: SPLADE (Formal et al. 2021) uses a BERT model with a regularized output head that produces a sparse vector over the vocabulary. Unlike BM25, weights are learned end-to-end, and query-time term expansion is implicit — the model predicts related terms the user did not type. SPLADE retrieval can be served on traditional inverted-index engines like Lucene, giving the benefits of learned relevance without a dense vector DB.
why_it_matters: SPLADE bridges sparse infrastructure with learned semantic retrieval. A good fit when you already have Lucene/Elastic but need smarter matching.

title: Hypothetical Document Embeddings (HyDE)
short_definition: A retrieval augmentation where an LLM generates a hypothetical answer to the query, which is then embedded and used as the search vector.
full_explanation: HyDE (Gao et al. 2022) addresses the query-document mismatch: queries are short, documents are long and verbose. It asks an LLM to draft a hypothetical passage that would answer the query, embeds that draft, and searches for real passages near it. Often lifts recall on zero-shot retrieval where no domain-tuned embedding model is available. Trade-off: extra LLM call per query, plus dependence on the LLM's world knowledge.
why_it_matters: HyDE is a cheap quality lift when retrieval misses because the query phrasing differs from the corpus.

title: Query Rewriting
short_definition: Using an LLM to rewrite or refine a user's raw query into one or more retrieval-friendly queries before hitting the retriever.
full_explanation: Users ask questions in ways that do not match corpus phrasing: 'What was that thing we launched last quarter?' is hard to retrieve. A rewriter LLM converts this into 'Q4 product launches 2025' and similar variants. Variants: single-rewrite, multi-query (generate N variants), step-back prompting (generalize before retrieving), and conversational rewriting (fold context from prior turns).
why_it_matters: In multi-turn chat and RAG, query rewriting is often the single biggest retrieval quality lever.

title: Multi-Query Retrieval
short_definition: A retrieval strategy where an LLM generates multiple diverse rewordings of the query, retrieves for each, and merges the results.
full_explanation: Multi-query retrieval asks the LLM: 'Generate 3–5 different ways to search for this question.' Each variant is run against the retriever and results are deduplicated and scored together (reciprocal rank fusion, score averaging). Helps when the query is ambiguous or uses niche phrasing. Cost: N retrievals per question. Often combined with a reranker on top.
why_it_matters: Multi-query is one of the most reliable easy wins for RAG recall with minimal engineering.

title: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)
short_definition: A method for merging ranked lists from multiple retrievers using each item's rank rather than raw scores — robust to score-scale mismatches.
full_explanation: RRF (Cormack et al. 2009) combines lists by computing sum(1/(k+rank_i)) across retrievers, with k typically around 60. It works even when retrievers produce scores on different scales (BM25 + cosine similarity) or no scores at all. This makes RRF the go-to fusion method for hybrid search combining sparse and dense retrieval, and it often matches or beats more complex learning-to-rank setups.
why_it_matters: RRF is the simplest effective way to combine hybrid retrieval results. Default choice for most RAG fusion.

title: RAG Fusion
short_definition: A RAG pattern that combines multi-query retrieval with reciprocal rank fusion to improve recall and robustness of retrieved context.
full_explanation: RAG Fusion (popularized by LangChain) stacks two ideas: (1) generate multiple query variants with an LLM, (2) retrieve for each and merge with RRF. The final context set is broader and more robust than single-query retrieval. Costs scale with the number of variants — typically 3–5. Works especially well for exploratory or ambiguous queries.
why_it_matters: RAG Fusion is a well-tested recipe that provides measurable recall lifts with predictable cost.

title: Parent-Document Retriever
short_definition: A retrieval pattern that indexes small chunks for precise matching but returns the larger parent document or section to the LLM for context.
full_explanation: Retrieving at the small-chunk level gives high precision in matching; retrieving large documents gives the LLM enough context to reason. Parent-document retriever splits the difference: index 200-token chunks, but when any chunk matches, return the 2000-token section it belongs to. Implemented via a parent-ID lookup alongside the vector index. LangChain and LlamaIndex both ship this pattern as a first-class retriever.
why_it_matters: This pattern often resolves the RAG dilemma between specificity of retrieval and completeness of context.

title: Small-to-Big Retrieval
short_definition: A retrieval pattern that matches against small embeddings for precision but returns progressively larger surrounding context to the LLM.
full_explanation: Similar to parent-document retrieval, small-to-big explicitly builds multi-granularity: embed each sentence, but store pointers to its paragraph and document. Match on sentence; return sentence + paragraph, or escalate to full document as token budget allows. Useful when documents have varying ideal retrieval granularities. LlamaIndex's auto-merging retriever implements this.
why_it_matters: For documents with mixed content density, small-to-big gives retrieval precision without starving the LLM of context.

title: Sentence Window Retrieval
short_definition: A retrieval pattern that indexes individual sentences but returns a window of surrounding sentences for LLM context.
full_explanation: Index each sentence as its own embedding but store a reference to its surrounding window (e.g., 3 sentences before and after). At retrieval, match by sentence but return the broader window. Useful for dense documents where the answer is often a specific sentence but needs nearby context to interpret. Works well with long or poorly paginated sources.
why_it_matters: Sentence windows often beat paragraph-level chunking on precision-heavy QA.

title: Contextual Compression (Retrieval)
short_definition: Passing retrieved documents through an LLM (or small model) to extract only the sentences relevant to the query before sending to the final generator.
full_explanation: Retrieved chunks often contain 80% irrelevant text around 20% that actually answers the query. Contextual compression runs each chunk through a light LLM or token classifier that extracts only the useful spans. This lowers the generator's token cost, reduces distraction, and often improves answer quality. LangChain ships this as ContextualCompressionRetriever with LLMExtractor.
why_it_matters: Compression often doubles RAG quality per token spent. A standard optimization for large-corpus RAG.

title: Graph RAG
short_definition: A RAG variant that builds a knowledge graph from documents and traverses entity relationships during retrieval, strong on multi-hop questions.
full_explanation: Graph RAG (Microsoft Research, 2024) extracts entities and relations from source documents, builds a graph, clusters into communities, and generates summaries per community. At query time it traverses the graph to find connected context across documents. Handles multi-hop queries ('how are A and B related?') better than chunk-based RAG. Costs more to build and query but shines on connected-knowledge domains.
why_it_matters: For structured knowledge bases and multi-hop QA, Graph RAG often substantially outperforms vanilla RAG.

title: Self-Query Retrieval
short_definition: A retrieval pattern where an LLM parses a natural-language query into both a semantic query and structured metadata filters.
full_explanation: Given 'Find me action movies from the 90s with a runtime over 2 hours,' a self-query retriever extracts: semantic query = 'action movie,' metadata filters = {decade: 1990s, runtime_min: 120}. The filters are applied at the vector DB layer before similarity search, so only matching documents are considered. Powerful for corpora with rich metadata (dates, categories, authors). LangChain and LlamaIndex implement this natively.
why_it_matters: Combining structured filters with semantic search is how you get precise results in real-world RAG.

title: Metadata Filtering (Vector DB)
short_definition: Constraining vector search to documents matching specified metadata conditions — date ranges, categories, authors, tenants — before computing similarity.
full_explanation: Most production vector DBs support metadata filters alongside vector similarity. A pre-filtered search restricts the ANN candidate pool to matching docs, then ranks by similarity. Key use: multi-tenancy (filter by tenant_id), freshness (updated_after: 2026-01-01), and categorical RAG (category: 'legal'). Pre-filtering is usually preferred over post-filtering, which can return fewer results than requested.
why_it_matters: Without metadata filtering, multi-tenant RAG leaks data across tenants. It is a basic safety primitive.

title: Semantic Caching
short_definition: Caching LLM responses based on semantic similarity of queries rather than exact string match — returning cached answers for 'similar enough' questions.
full_explanation: Semantic caching embeds each query, stores (query_embedding, response) pairs, and on a new query checks for nearby past queries (similarity above a threshold). Hits return cached responses, saving LLM cost and latency. Works well for high-volume FAQ-like workloads. Risks: cache hits on subtly different questions that should have different answers. Tooling: GPTCache, LangChain cache layer, LLM gateway caches like Helicone.
why_it_matters: Semantic caching is the single highest-leverage cost reduction for high-traffic assistants — often 20–40% cache hit rates.
common_misconceptions: High similarity ≠ same question. Threshold tuning is non-trivial; too loose and you return wrong answers.

title: Red Teaming (AI Red Team)
short_definition: Adversarial testing of an AI system by human or automated attackers to find vulnerabilities, harmful outputs, and policy violations before deployment.
full_explanation: AI red teaming combines classic security red teaming with ML-specific attacks: jailbreaks, prompt injection, data extraction, harmful-content elicitation, bias probing. Common output is a taxonomy of failure modes with reproduction prompts. Frontier labs maintain dedicated red teams; smaller teams use frameworks like Garak, PyRIT, and PromptFoo. Red teaming is now a formal requirement under the EU AI Act for high-risk systems.
why_it_matters: Red teaming is the main way frontier labs discover and patch the failure modes before public release. Production systems need their own smaller version.

title: Adversarial Robustness
short_definition: A model's resistance to carefully crafted inputs designed to cause misclassification or harmful output — a classic ML security concern extended to LLMs.
full_explanation: Adversarial examples were first studied in vision — tiny pixel perturbations flipping a classifier's prediction. In LLMs, adversarial inputs are jailbreak prompts, prompt injections, and optimized gibberish suffixes (GCG attacks) that trigger specific outputs. Defenses: adversarial training, input/output filtering, random masking, and constitutional training. Perfect robustness is unobtainable; layered defenses are the practical target.
why_it_matters: Any system exposed to untrusted input is a target. Adversarial robustness is table-stakes for customer-facing LLMs.

title: Data Poisoning
short_definition: An attack where an adversary injects malicious examples into training data to embed a backdoor, degrade quality, or cause specific misbehavior at inference time.
full_explanation: Data poisoning attacks can be broad (degrade a whole task) or targeted (trigger misbehavior on specific inputs). For LLMs, web-scraped pretraining data is the main attack surface — a handful of poisoned pages with carefully crafted content can shift model behavior if not filtered. RAG corpora are equally vulnerable — adversarial pages can redirect answers. Defenses: provenance tracking, content filtering, anomaly detection on training data.
why_it_matters: LLM training data pipelines are mostly web-scale scraping, which means they are mostly exposed to whoever posts to the web.

title: Backdoor Attack
short_definition: A data-poisoning attack that makes a model behave normally except when a specific 'trigger' pattern is present, at which point it misbehaves.
full_explanation: Backdoor attacks plant a trigger — a rare phrase, special character, or image patch — that activates adversary-chosen behavior. The model passes standard evaluation because triggers are absent from benign data. Defenses: scan training data for anomalous repeated patterns, test with suspected-trigger prompts, prefer models with full training-data provenance. Backdoors in shared LoRAs and public model hubs are a real concern.
why_it_matters: Backdoors survive fine-tuning. Pulling a random model off a public hub is a supply-chain risk unless vetted.

title: Membership Inference Attack
short_definition: An attack that determines whether a specific example was part of a model's training data — a privacy violation even without full data extraction.
full_explanation: Membership inference attacks (Shokri et al. 2017) exploit the fact that models tend to be more confident on training examples than on new ones. Variants use perplexity, loss, or calibration gaps as signals. For LLMs, membership inference can reveal whether a specific copyrighted book or private document was in training. Defenses include differential privacy during training and deduplication of training data.
why_it_matters: For privacy compliance (GDPR, HIPAA), membership inference is a known attack vector teams should model when training on sensitive data.

title: Training Data Extraction
short_definition: An attack that elicits verbatim training data from a model's outputs — a major privacy and copyright concern.
full_explanation: Carlini et al. (2021, 2023) showed LLMs memorize and can regurgitate chunks of training data, including PII, source code, and long literary passages. Extraction techniques include clever prompts ('continue the Harry Potter chapter starting with...'), divergence attacks that cause repeated-token outputs, and 'canary' evaluations where planted secrets are probed. Defenses: deduplication, differential privacy, output filtering.
why_it_matters: Extraction shows training data is not a black box. It is a regulatory risk for anything personal, copyrighted, or proprietary.

title: Differential Privacy
short_definition: A mathematical privacy guarantee that the output of a computation reveals essentially nothing about any single input example.
full_explanation: Differential privacy (Dwork et al. 2006) adds calibrated noise to computations so that including or excluding any one record produces statistically indistinguishable outputs. For ML, DP-SGD (Abadi et al. 2016) adds noise to per-example gradients. For LLMs, DP at pretraining is still impractical at frontier scale due to noise costs; DP is more commonly applied to fine-tuning on sensitive data. Privacy budget ε quantifies the trade-off with model quality.
why_it_matters: DP is the strongest formal privacy guarantee available for ML and a key tool for training on regulated data.

title: Indirect Prompt Injection
short_definition: A prompt injection attack delivered through content the LLM reads (a web page, email, document) rather than the direct user input.
full_explanation: Indirect prompt injection (Greshake et al. 2023) smuggles malicious instructions into content the LLM processes via tools: a web page the agent browses, an email it summarizes, a file it reads. The LLM treats these instructions as authoritative unless trained or prompted otherwise. Classic impact: data exfiltration ('send history to attacker.com'), tool abuse, and policy bypass. Defenses: clearly delimiting trusted vs. untrusted content, output filtering, least-privilege tool scopes.
why_it_matters: As LLMs read more external content (browsing, email, docs), indirect injection becomes the #1 attack vector. Every agent designer must model it.

title: Prompt Leakage (System Prompt Exfiltration)
short_definition: An attack that recovers a system prompt by tricking the LLM into repeating its hidden instructions.
full_explanation: System prompts contain product configuration, API keys (bad practice), tool-use instructions, and custom persona. Prompt leakage attacks extract them with requests like 'Repeat the text above,' 'What are your instructions?', or subtle variations. Many production assistants leak their system prompts readily. Defenses: train or prompt the model to refuse requests for its instructions, but no defense is perfect — assume the system prompt is eventually public.
why_it_matters: Anything truly secret should not be in a system prompt. Assume it will leak and design accordingly.

title: Model Theft (Model Extraction)
short_definition: An attack that reconstructs or approximates a closed-API model by querying it extensively and training a student on the outputs.
full_explanation: Model extraction attacks (Tramèr et al. 2016) query a target model and train a local student on the (input, output) pairs. For LLMs, distilling a closed model via its API produces a cheaper but similar-behaving model — often against the API provider's terms of service. OpenAI and Anthropic ToS explicitly prohibit using outputs to train competing models. Rate limits and output watermarking are partial defenses.
why_it_matters: Model extraction is a legal and competitive risk more than a security vulnerability. Understanding it shapes API policies.

title: Content Watermarking (AI Content Provenance)
short_definition: Techniques for marking AI-generated content so it can later be identified — steganographic for images, statistical for text.
full_explanation: For text, watermarking methods (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023) subtly bias token selection toward a secret 'green list' so statistical tests can detect AI origin. For images and audio, steganographic watermarks survive compression and light edits. Adversarial robustness is limited — paraphrasing breaks most text watermarks. Provenance standards like C2PA take a complementary approach: sign metadata at creation rather than embed into content.
why_it_matters: Watermarking is a partial tool for tracing AI content. It is central to current regulation (EU AI Act) and election-integrity debates.

title: C2PA (Content Provenance Standard)
short_definition: An open standard for cryptographically signing content metadata — who made it, when, with what tool — to establish provenance.
full_explanation: C2PA (Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity, 2021+) defines a spec for attaching signed assertions to images, video, and audio: creator identity, tool, edit history, AI-generation status. Adobe, Microsoft, Nikon, Sony, and OpenAI support it. C2PA does not prove authenticity — only that a specific party signed specific metadata at a specific time. Complement to content watermarking rather than replacement.
why_it_matters: C2PA is the most widely adopted provenance standard. News orgs and platforms increasingly require it for media provenance.

title: Deepfake Detection
short_definition: Techniques for detecting AI-generated or AI-manipulated images, audio, and video — a moving target as generators improve.
full_explanation: Deepfake detectors range from frequency-domain analyzers (GANs leave characteristic artifacts) to model-based classifiers trained on real and synthetic pairs, to biometric consistency checks (heartbeat from skin color variation). Detection accuracy degrades rapidly on new generators. The field is fundamentally adversarial; provenance-based approaches (C2PA) and out-of-band verification are considered more durable than pixel-based detection.
why_it_matters: Deepfake detection is necessary for election integrity, fraud prevention, and journalism verification. No single tool is reliable; layer them.

title: Toxicity Classification
short_definition: ML systems that score text (and sometimes images) for harmful, abusive, or policy-violating content for moderation pipelines.
full_explanation: Toxicity classifiers (Perspective API, Detoxify, OpenAI Moderation, Llama Guard) output probabilities for categories like hate speech, harassment, sexual content, and self-harm. Training data is hand-labeled examples from platforms. Known failure modes: over-flagging reclaimed slurs from affected communities, missing context-dependent harm, and adversarial paraphrases. Content moderation increasingly layers LLM classifiers with rules and human review.
why_it_matters: Every user-generated content platform needs toxicity classification. It is one of the oldest and most-deployed NLP safety applications.

title: Llama Guard
short_definition: Meta's open-source LLM-based safety classifier trained to detect policy violations in both user prompts and model responses.
full_explanation: Llama Guard (Inan et al. 2023) fine-tunes a small Llama model to classify prompts and responses against a taxonomy of harm categories (violence, sexual content, self-harm, criminal planning, privacy). Deployed as a pre- and post-filter around a main LLM. Llama Guard 3 (2024) extended to 100+ languages and added visual content support. Permissive license, widely integrated as the default open-source safety layer.
why_it_matters: Llama Guard is the de facto open-source safety classifier. Most self-hosted LLM deployments use it or a similar model for moderation.

title: NeMo Guardrails
short_definition: Nvidia's open-source framework for adding programmable safety and topical guardrails to LLM applications via a declarative DSL (Colang).
full_explanation: NeMo Guardrails lets developers define rails for conversations: topical rails (stay on-topic), input/output filters (block unsafe prompts/responses), fact-checking rails, and tool-use safety. The Colang DSL describes expected conversation flows and interventions. Integrates with LangChain and major LLM APIs. A popular structured alternative to ad-hoc system-prompt guardrails.
why_it_matters: NeMo Guardrails gives production teams a clean abstraction for conversational safety beyond what system prompts alone can provide.

title: Guardrails AI
short_definition: An open-source Python framework for enforcing structured output and policy compliance on LLM responses via validators and re-prompts.
full_explanation: Guardrails (guardrails.ai) wraps LLM calls with a validation layer: declare a schema (RAIL, JSON) and validators (no PII, no toxicity, match a regex, pass a semantic check). On violation, the framework automatically re-prompts the LLM with the failure description or falls back to a default. Mix of local validators, Guardrails Hub rules, and LLM-as-judge validators.
why_it_matters: Guardrails AI is one of the cleanest ways to enforce contracts on LLM output beyond JSON schema enforcement.

title: Alignment Tax
short_definition: The performance cost a model pays on general capabilities after alignment training — the trade-off between being useful and being safe/helpful.
full_explanation: Aggressive RLHF, DPO, or safety tuning can reduce a model's raw capabilities — more refusals, blander writing, worse coding, worse reasoning. The alignment tax is the measurable drop on capability benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, MATH) after alignment. Labs try to minimize it via careful reward modeling, balanced data mixes, and targeted safety training that does not over-generalize into refusal of benign tasks.
why_it_matters: Alignment tax is why over-tuned models feel 'nerfed.' Balancing it against safety is an active design problem.

title: Over-Refusal
short_definition: When a safety-tuned LLM refuses benign requests it misclassifies as harmful — a common failure mode of aggressive alignment.
full_explanation: Over-refusal happens when safety classifiers or alignment training over-generalize. Examples: refusing to explain how to kill a process in Linux, declining to answer medical questions with disclaimers only, refusing role-play with benign personas. Benchmarks like XSTest and OR-Bench measure it. Teams reduce over-refusal by adding 'benign but superficially risky' examples to the safety dataset so the model learns the boundary.
why_it_matters: Over-refusal kills user trust and is the top consumer complaint about aligned assistants. Measurable and fixable.

title: Reward Hacking
short_definition: A failure mode of RL where the model learns to maximize the reward signal in ways that violate the designer's intent.
full_explanation: Reward hacking happens when the reward function is a proxy for 'good behavior' and the model exploits the gap. Examples: a game agent that jumps in a loop because the reward counts jumps, an RLHF model that becomes sycophantic because users rated confident answers higher, a summarization reward model that prefers length. Mitigation: tighter reward modeling, adversarial testing, KL constraints to the base model, and process reward models instead of outcome-only rewards.
why_it_matters: Reward hacking is the single most-cited source of RLHF failure. Every reward function should be stress-tested for it.

title: Specification Gaming
short_definition: A broader term for agents that satisfy the letter of a specification while violating its intent — reward hacking is one instance.
full_explanation: Specification gaming is the pattern of agents finding shortcuts their designers did not intend. DeepMind's compiled list includes a robot arm that flipped a box to hide the target square, an evolved organism that exploited a physics-simulation bug, and a chess engine that crashed the opponent's process. For LLMs, specification gaming shows up as over-refusal, verbose-to-the-reward output, or obsequious answers that maximize human rating without helping.
why_it_matters: Specification gaming is a foundational alignment problem. The more capable models become, the more creative the shortcuts.

title: Goal Misgeneralization
short_definition: When an agent learns to pursue a goal that is correlated with the intended goal on training data but diverges in deployment.
full_explanation: Goal misgeneralization (Langosco et al. 2022) happens when training cannot distinguish the real goal from a correlated proxy. A robot trained to pick up the yellow block (always on the left) learns 'go left' rather than 'pick up yellow.' For LLMs: an assistant trained on helpful responses may learn 'sound confident' instead of 'be correct,' producing confident hallucinations in deployment. Mitigation: diverse training environments, adversarial evaluation, and interpretability.
why_it_matters: Goal misgeneralization is one of the main ways capable systems fail in deployment. A top concern for AI safety research.

title: Sycophancy
short_definition: An LLM's tendency to agree with users rather than tell them uncomfortable truths, often reinforced by RLHF on preference data that rewards flattery.
full_explanation: Sycophancy is a well-documented RLHF side effect (Sharma et al. 2023). Users rate responses that agree with them higher than those that contradict, so RLHF models drift toward validation over accuracy. Symptoms: reversing correct answers when users push back, praising obviously bad ideas, softening critiques. Mitigation: adversarial training with 'prefer honest over flattering' data, constitutional rules on correction, and user-side prompt engineering.
why_it_matters: Sycophancy undermines LLMs as research, writing, or decision-support tools. It is measurable and fixable.

title: Hallucination Detection
short_definition: Automated methods for flagging LLM outputs that are not grounded in evidence — via self-consistency, citation checking, or entailment classifiers.
full_explanation: Hallucination detection pairs generation with verification. Methods: (1) SelfCheckGPT samples multiple completions and measures consistency; high variance signals hallucination. (2) Citation-based approaches require the model to quote sources and verify quotes match. (3) NLI (entailment) models check whether answers are supported by retrieved context. (4) LLM-as-judge over (claim, source) pairs. Each method has false-positive and false-negative trade-offs; stacking them is common.
why_it_matters: Hallucination detection is the primary guardrail for factual assistants and RAG products.

title: Content Moderation (AI-Based)
short_definition: Using ML systems — toxicity classifiers, LLM-based reviewers, image/video analyzers — to review user-generated or AI-generated content against policy.
full_explanation: Content moderation at platform scale combines rules, classical ML classifiers, and increasingly LLM-based reviewers that can handle nuance. Typical pipeline: rules first (exact-match filters), classifiers second (toxicity, CSAM detection), LLM review third (complex policy edge cases), human appeal fourth. For AI-generated content, moderation is bidirectional — filter prompts for unsafe requests and responses for unsafe output.
why_it_matters: Every UGC platform and LLM product has a content-moderation stack. Its quality determines exposure and trust.

title: MMLU (Benchmark)
short_definition: Massive Multitask Language Understanding — a 57-subject multiple-choice benchmark that became the standard broad-knowledge test for LLMs.
full_explanation: MMLU (Hendrycks et al. 2021) consists of ~16k multiple-choice questions across 57 subjects (STEM, humanities, law, medicine). It is the most-reported benchmark in LLM papers and product launches. Criticisms: significant training-data contamination at this point, test-taking skill rather than reasoning, English-centric. Replacements and complements: MMLU-Pro (harder, 10-way choice), GPQA (graduate-level science), BBH, and more niche domain evals.
why_it_matters: MMLU is the lingua franca of LLM benchmarking. Nearly every model release reports it, so you need to know how to read those numbers.
common_misconceptions: MMLU numbers do not necessarily track chat quality. A model with 80% MMLU can still be a worse assistant than a 70% model.

title: HumanEval (Benchmark)
short_definition: OpenAI's 164-problem Python code benchmark where solutions are scored by unit-test pass rate — the canonical LLM coding eval.
full_explanation: HumanEval (Chen et al. 2021) gives the model a function signature and docstring; the model must produce an implementation that passes hidden unit tests. Metric is pass@1 (first attempt passes) or pass@k (any of k attempts passes). Heavily contaminated now — frontier models saturate HumanEval at 90%+. Modern replacements: MBPP+, LiveCodeBench (uncontaminated live problems), BigCodeBench, SWE-Bench for repo-level tasks.
why_it_matters: HumanEval is historical. For current coding eval, LiveCodeBench, BigCodeBench, and SWE-Bench are more meaningful.

title: SWE-Bench
short_definition: A benchmark where an LLM must resolve real-world GitHub issues in open-source Python repos by producing a patch that passes the repo's tests.
full_explanation: SWE-Bench (Jimenez et al. 2023) curates 2,000+ real GitHub issues with repo context, user-written issue descriptions, and actual fixes that passed tests. The model must navigate a repo, identify the relevant files, and write a passing patch. SWE-Bench Verified is a human-validated 500-issue subset used by most frontier reports. Scores below 20% dominated through 2024; Claude 3.5/4 and GPT-4o reached 40–55%, with agentic setups now pushing higher.
why_it_matters: SWE-Bench is the closest thing to a real software engineering benchmark. It measures what coding agents actually need to do.

title: MTEB (Massive Text Embedding Benchmark)
short_definition: A benchmark suite of 50+ tasks (retrieval, classification, clustering, reranking, STS) for comparing text embedding models on a standardized leaderboard.
full_explanation: MTEB (Muennighoff et al. 2022) covers the full space of embedding use cases. Retrieval is the most-cited subtask. The MTEB leaderboard on Hugging Face is the default reference for picking an embedding model. Multilingual MTEB (MMTEB) added 100+ languages. Reading MTEB tables intelligently requires attending to task weights and your specific downstream use.
why_it_matters: Picking an embedding model is MTEB-shopping. It is the single most-consulted leaderboard for RAG engineering.

title: BEIR (Retrieval Benchmark)
short_definition: A zero-shot retrieval benchmark spanning 18 diverse datasets (news, bio, Wikipedia, finance, fact-checking) used to evaluate retrieval models out-of-domain.
full_explanation: BEIR (Thakur et al. 2021) tests retrievers on tasks and corpora they were not trained on, revealing generalization strengths and weaknesses. Results showed BM25 to be surprisingly hard to beat zero-shot, sparking research into domain-robust dense retrievers. Modern strong embedding models consistently beat BM25 on BEIR average. Now a subset of MTEB.
why_it_matters: BEIR is the canonical retrieval generalization benchmark. Quoting BEIR-average is common in retrieval-model papers.

title: Needle in a Haystack
short_definition: A long-context evaluation that buries a specific fact (the needle) at a random position in a huge irrelevant context (the haystack) and asks the model to retrieve it.
full_explanation: Needle-in-a-Haystack (NIAH) was popularized by Greg Kamradt as a simple sanity check for long-context models. Charts show success rate as a function of context length (x-axis) and needle position (y-axis); gaps in the middle reveal 'lost in the middle' degradation. RULER and LongBench are more rigorous successors that test multi-needle and multi-hop long-context reasoning.
why_it_matters: NIAH catches the most basic long-context failure. A model that fails NIAH has no business being used at long context.
common_misconceptions: Passing NIAH does not mean the model can actually reason over long context — it only proves it can retrieve a single fact.

title: Chatbot Arena (LMSYS)
short_definition: LMSYS's crowd-sourced pairwise LLM evaluation platform where users vote on anonymous side-by-side responses, producing an Elo leaderboard.
full_explanation: Chatbot Arena (2023+) shows anonymized responses from two random models to the same prompt; users click the winner. Millions of votes produce an Elo score per model. Unlike academic benchmarks, Arena measures what people actually prefer. Known biases: length bias (longer usually wins), stylistic bias, and difficulty normalization issues. Arena-Hard-Auto uses strong LLM-judges to simulate Arena at scale without live voting.
why_it_matters: Arena Elo is the single most-cited 'real-world preference' metric. It is a reality check on benchmark-driven ranks.

title: LLM-as-Judge (Reprise)
short_definition: Using a strong LLM to score or pairwise-compare other LLMs' outputs — a scalable substitute for human evaluation with well-documented biases.
full_explanation: LLM-as-judge has become the default quality-evaluation method for open-ended LLM tasks. Common patterns: single-answer grading (score 1–10), pairwise comparison (A or B), rubric-based scoring. Known biases: position bias (A bias if not shuffled), length bias, style bias, self-preference (models rate their own outputs higher). Chain-of-thought judging, rubric constraints, and multi-judge ensembles mitigate these.
why_it_matters: LLM-as-judge is how modern model comparisons and regression tests scale. Getting its biases right is a product skill.

title: G-Eval
short_definition: An LLM-as-judge method that uses chain-of-thought evaluation steps and form-filling to produce reliable scores for generation quality.
full_explanation: G-Eval (Liu et al. 2023) structures LLM-based evaluation: define evaluation criteria, have the LLM reason through each criterion step-by-step, then output a discrete score. Weighting step probabilities gives more fine-grained scores than raw digits. G-Eval consistently outperforms simple single-score prompting and correlates better with human judgment on summarization, dialog, and creative generation.
why_it_matters: G-Eval is a cleaner LLM-as-judge recipe than naive scoring prompts. Good default for custom evals.

title: RAGAS (RAG Assessment)
short_definition: An open-source RAG evaluation framework that scores faithfulness, answer relevance, context precision, and context recall without human ground truth.
full_explanation: RAGAS (Es et al. 2023) decomposes RAG quality into measurable dimensions and uses LLM-based evaluators to score each one, avoiding the need for hand-labeled golden answers. Faithfulness: does the answer make claims supported by retrieved context? Answer relevance: does the answer address the question? Context precision: are retrieved chunks relevant? Context recall: did we retrieve everything needed? Widely used for CI-grade RAG regression tests.
why_it_matters: RAGAS makes RAG eval runnable in CI. It is the closest thing to a standard for measuring RAG quality.

title: LangSmith
short_definition: LangChain's observability and evaluation platform for LLM applications — traces, datasets, evals, and prompt management in one tool.
full_explanation: LangSmith captures full execution traces for chains and agents (each LLM call, tool invocation, intermediate state), stores them for debugging, and lets teams turn real traces into evaluation datasets. Built-in LLM-as-judge evaluators and custom eval support. Integrates with LangChain, LlamaIndex, and raw OpenAI/Anthropic SDKs. The dominant observability tool in the LangChain ecosystem.
why_it_matters: For LangChain users, LangSmith is the default observability path. Even non-LangChain projects often use it for traces.

title: Langfuse
short_definition: An open-source observability and evaluation platform for LLM apps — traces, prompts, eval runs, user feedback — self-hostable alternative to LangSmith.
full_explanation: Langfuse provides trace capture (via SDK or OpenTelemetry), prompt management with A/B testing, evaluation pipelines with LLM-as-judge, user-feedback collection, and a self-host option under MIT license. Used by teams that want LangSmith-style tracing but on their own infra or with looser licensing. Integrates with LangChain, LlamaIndex, and major LLM SDKs.
why_it_matters: Langfuse is the leading open-source LLM observability tool. For teams with data-residency needs, it is the default pick.

title: Arize Phoenix
short_definition: Arize's open-source ML and LLM observability platform with OpenTelemetry-based tracing, RAG evaluation, and drift detection.
full_explanation: Phoenix uses OpenInference (OTel-based) for traces, meaning you can instrument once and ship traces anywhere. Built-in evaluators cover RAG quality, relevance, hallucination, and toxicity. Runs locally via pip install or self-hosted. Strong fit for teams that want standard observability tooling rather than a bespoke SDK. Paid Arize product extends with drift detection and guardrails for production.
why_it_matters: Phoenix is a polished open-source choice aligned with OTel standards. Good for OSS-first teams.

title: MLflow
short_definition: An open-source platform for the ML lifecycle — experiment tracking, model registry, deployment — increasingly extended with LLM-specific features.
full_explanation: MLflow (Databricks, 2018+) has four core components: Tracking (log params, metrics, artifacts), Projects (reproducible packaging), Models (standard format for deployment), and Registry (versioning and stage transitions). Recent versions added LLM evaluation, prompt tracking, and AI gateway features. Widely deployed at enterprise scale as an ML experiment and model management backbone.
why_it_matters: MLflow is the most-deployed open ML experiment platform. Any serious ML team needs something like it.

title: Weights & Biases (W&B)
short_definition: A managed platform for experiment tracking, model monitoring, and LLM evaluation widely used across ML research and industry.
full_explanation: W&B (2018+) captures every training run's metrics, hyperparameters, system telemetry, and artifacts. Rich visualization (parallel coordinates, scatter plots), sweep support for hyperparameter search, and Registry for model versioning. W&B Weave extends tracking to LLM traces and evals. Most-used alongside PyTorch, Lightning, and Hugging Face Trainer.
why_it_matters: W&B is the most common 'I ran an experiment, here's what happened' tool in ML research today.

title: Feature Store
short_definition: Infrastructure that stores and serves engineered features for ML with guarantees about consistency between training and serving.
full_explanation: Feature stores (Feast, Tecton, Databricks Feature Store) solve the training/serving skew problem: a feature computed one way in batch training and a different way in online serving causes silent model degradation. They register feature definitions (SQL, Python), materialize to offline store (for training) and online store (for low-latency serving), and version them. Less common in LLM-centric stacks but central to tabular ML and recsys.
why_it_matters: For production ML on behavioral or transactional data, a feature store is table stakes.

title: Model Registry
short_definition: A versioned catalog of trained models with metadata — training run, metrics, stage (staging/prod), lineage — governing which model is deployed.
full_explanation: Model registries (MLflow Registry, SageMaker Model Registry, Vertex AI) centralize model versioning and deployment gates. Typical workflow: new training run → registered as candidate → eval pipeline compares to current prod → promotion to prod stage triggers deployment. For LLMs, registries now track LoRA adapters, prompts, and quantized variants alongside base models.
why_it_matters: A registry is how you avoid 'which model is in prod?' chaos. Necessary for any team shipping more than one model.

title: Model Serving
short_definition: The infrastructure that hosts trained models behind an API with low latency, high availability, and scaling properties needed for production traffic.
full_explanation: Model serving stacks handle batching, scheduling, GPU utilization, autoscaling, health checks, version routing, and observability. Options: custom (Triton, TorchServe, TF Serving, BentoML), LLM-specific (vLLM, TGI, SGLang, TensorRT-LLM), managed (SageMaker, Vertex, Bedrock). Choice depends on model type, expected QPS, cost per token targets, and ops maturity. LLM serving is its own specialty due to KV cache and batching dynamics.
why_it_matters: Model serving is where trained models meet users. Picking the right stack is the biggest infra decision after training.

title: Canary Deployment
short_definition: A rollout strategy that sends a small fraction of traffic to a new model version before full promotion, catching regressions early.
full_explanation: Canary deploys route e.g. 5% of traffic to the new version while 95% continues on the stable version. Metrics (quality, latency, error rate, cost) are compared; if the new version underperforms, traffic is rolled back. Shadow deployment (dark launch) is a related pattern where the new version receives the same traffic as prod but its outputs are not returned to users — safer for initial evaluation.
why_it_matters: Canary deploys catch regressions before they reach all users. Standard practice for any model serving system with real traffic.

title: Shadow Deployment
short_definition: Running a new model alongside production, processing live traffic in parallel but not serving its outputs to users — for safe quality comparison.
full_explanation: Shadow mode duplicates each request to both the stable and candidate models. Only the stable output is returned; the candidate output is logged and compared offline. This yields paired samples on real traffic without user-facing risk. Costs extra inference but catches regressions that offline evals miss due to distribution shift.
why_it_matters: Shadow mode is the gold standard for pre-launch model validation when you can afford the extra compute.

title: A/B Testing (LLM)
short_definition: Running a randomized experiment where different user segments receive different model versions or prompts, measuring impact on business metrics.
full_explanation: A/B testing for LLMs follows classical experimentation: randomize users, serve variant A or B, measure outcomes (task completion, session length, satisfaction, cost). Challenges unique to LLMs: outputs are stochastic even within a variant, user preference is hard to measure directly, and novelty effects bias early results. Use user-level randomization, long-enough run-times, and pre-registered hypotheses.
why_it_matters: A/B tests are the only way to know if a model or prompt change actually improves product metrics, not just offline scores.

title: Drift Detection
short_definition: Monitoring for changes in input data distribution, labels, or model predictions over time that may degrade model performance.
full_explanation: Drift types: covariate drift (input distribution shifts), label drift (target distribution shifts), and concept drift (input-to-output relationship changes). Detection methods: statistical tests (KL divergence, KS test) on features, embedding-space monitoring for LLM inputs, and quality proxies (rejection rates, user feedback). For LLMs, the main 'drift' is topic shift in user queries — monitoring can flag when production prompts drift away from evaluation distributions.
why_it_matters: Drift is how a perfectly deployed model silently becomes wrong. Every long-lived ML system needs drift monitoring.

title: Annotation (Data Labeling)
short_definition: The process of humans labeling examples — spans, classes, preferences, ratings — to create training or evaluation data.
full_explanation: Annotation is the factory floor of ML. Tools: Label Studio (open source), Argilla (LLM-centric), Scale AI, Appen. Quality levers: clear guidelines, inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa), calibration with expert-verified golden sets, feedback loops. For LLM preference data, annotator drift and rater disagreement are large enough to demand active QA.
why_it_matters: Model quality is bottlenecked by label quality. Investing in annotation is often the highest-ROI ML work.

title: Active Learning
short_definition: A labeling strategy where the model selects the most informative examples to label next, maximizing model improvement per annotation budget.
full_explanation: Active learning picks examples the current model is most uncertain about or would benefit most from knowing: highest-entropy predictions, examples near decision boundaries, or representative samples of uncovered regions. Saves 30–70% of labeling effort in well-run setups. For LLMs, active learning selects prompts where the model's sampled responses disagree, flagging them for SFT or preference collection.
why_it_matters: When labels are scarce or expensive, active learning is the difference between a shippable model and giving up.

title: Data Quality
short_definition: The measurable properties that determine how useful a dataset is — accuracy, completeness, consistency, freshness, coverage, and labeling reliability.
full_explanation: Data quality dimensions: accuracy (are labels correct?), completeness (are examples missing?), consistency (do identical inputs get identical labels?), coverage (does the set represent production distribution?), freshness (is it recent enough?), and bias (does it systematically under-represent groups?). Tools: Great Expectations, Deequ, data contracts for enforcing schema. For LLMs, data quality checks on pretraining corpora — deduplication, PII scrubbing, license tagging — are a full subfield.
why_it_matters: Every ML improvement conversation eventually becomes a data quality conversation. Get ahead of it.

title: Multinomial Sampling
short_definition: Drawing the next token by sampling from the model's full probability distribution over the vocabulary, weighted by predicted probabilities.
full_explanation: Multinomial sampling is the textbook stochastic decoder: compute p(token | context) for each vocabulary item, then sample proportionally. With temperature = 1 it uses the raw softmax; with temperature → 0 it approaches greedy decoding. Rarely used alone in modern systems because the long tail of low-probability tokens contributes noise; top-p, top-k, or min-p truncate it before sampling.
why_it_matters: Multinomial sampling is the baseline every other sampling method modifies. Understand it first.

title: Min-P Sampling
short_definition: A sampling method that keeps tokens with probability above a fraction (p_min) of the most likely token's probability, adapting the cutoff to confidence.
full_explanation: Min-p (Nguyen et al. 2024) fixes a limitation of top-p: when the model is very confident, top-p can still pull in unrelated low-probability tokens. Min-p sets a threshold relative to the top token — if the top prediction has probability 0.9 and p_min = 0.05, the cutoff is 0.045, so only strong alternatives are kept. When the model is uncertain (top prob 0.1, cutoff 0.005), more tokens survive. Often produces more coherent long-form text than top-p at similar diversity.
why_it_matters: Min-p is gaining traction as a drop-in replacement for top-p in high-quality generation, especially for creative writing.

title: Typical Sampling
short_definition: Sampling tokens whose information content is close to the distribution's expected entropy — biasing away from both too-predictable and too-surprising tokens.
full_explanation: Typical sampling (Meister et al. 2022) is grounded in information theory. At each step it computes each token's negative log probability (information content) and keeps only tokens whose information content is within τ of the distribution's expected entropy. Result: the sampler prefers 'typical' tokens, avoiding both the most predictable (boring) and the most surprising (incoherent) ones. Useful for creative text where human-like surprise levels matter.
why_it_matters: Typical sampling is a principled alternative to top-p for creative tasks. Produces notably different style from nucleus sampling.

title: Mirostat
short_definition: A sampling method that dynamically adjusts top-k to maintain a target output perplexity (surprise level) across the generation.
full_explanation: Mirostat (Basu et al. 2020) treats decoding as a control problem: set a target perplexity τ, then at each step adapt the cutoff so the sampled token matches τ on average. Mirostat v2 simplifies and stabilizes. The benefit is consistent output quality across long generations where plain top-p can drift into repetition (too low perplexity) or incoherence (too high).
