short_definition: The distinction between 'open weights' (model files released, often under usage restrictions) and 'open source' (training code, data, and process also available).
full_explanation: 'Open source' in software means source code plus license freedom (OSI-approved licenses). For AI, most 'open' models are only open weights — the trained file is downloadable but training code, data, and recipes remain private. OSI's 2024 Open Source AI Definition requires releasing model parameters plus sufficient training information to recreate a similar model. True OSI-compliant open-source AI remains rare (OLMo, Pythia, some Nomic models); Llama, Qwen, Gemma, and Mistral are open-weights but not OSI open-source.
why_it_matters: 'Open source AI' marketing often overreaches. Knowing the distinction matters for reproducibility, auditability, and licensing decisions.

title: License (AI Model Licenses)
short_definition: The legal terms governing how a model's weights can be used — ranging from permissive (Apache 2.0, MIT) to restricted (Llama Community License, research-only) to custom.
full_explanation: Common AI model licenses: Apache 2.0 / MIT (fully permissive — Qwen, Mistral 7B, StarCoder), Llama Community License (permissive but some restrictions for very large deployments), Gemma Terms (permissive with Responsible AI clauses), RAIL (Responsible AI License — restricts harmful uses), research-only (CC-BY-NC, OpenRAIL), and custom 'open weights' terms with use-case prohibitions. License compatibility matters when fine-tunes inherit parent licensing.
why_it_matters: Model choice is half capability, half licensing. For commercial deployment, license diligence is mandatory.

title: Supply Chain Risk (AI)
short_definition: The risk that downloaded models, datasets, or training pipelines contain backdoors, poisoned data, or undisclosed behaviors — a growing concern as open-weights use expands.
full_explanation: AI supply chain risks: (1) poisoned training data in public corpora, (2) backdoors in community fine-tunes or LoRAs, (3) malicious code in model loading scripts (pickle vulnerabilities — one reason for safetensors), (4) compromised package registries, (5) prompt-injection payloads in third-party datasets. Mitigations: trusted model sources, safetensors format, scanning via tools like ModelScan, vendor due diligence, and preferring models with full provenance.
why_it_matters: As open-model use scales, supply-chain attacks become realistic. Governance needs to catch up.

title: AI Observability (Reprise)
short_definition: The practice of monitoring LLM and ML systems in production — traces, cost, quality, safety events, drift — to ensure reliability and continuous improvement.
full_explanation: AI observability extends traditional observability (logs, metrics, traces) with LLM-specific signals: token usage, cost per request, prompt/response content (with privacy controls), tool-use patterns, semantic drift, quality evals run over production samples, and safety-rule triggers. Platforms: Langfuse, LangSmith, Arize Phoenix, Helicone, Dynatrace/Datadog LLM observability products. OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions standardize the data model.
why_it_matters: Without observability, you cannot debug, improve, or trust LLM systems in production. Essential investment for any serious deployment.

title: LLM Router / Model Routing
short_definition: A pattern that dispatches each query to the most cost-effective model capable of handling it — cheap model for easy queries, expensive model only when needed.
full_explanation: Routers classify incoming queries (by heuristic, classifier, or LLM-as-judge) and send each to an appropriate model. Examples: simple lookup to small Mistral, complex reasoning to Claude Opus. Projects: RouteLLM, Martian, Unify, Not Diamond. Savings are often 50–90% while preserving quality on hard queries. The main challenge is building a router that does not become a worse bottleneck than the models.
why_it_matters: For high-volume LLM products, model routing is one of the biggest cost levers available.

title: Token Per Second (TPS)
short_definition: A throughput metric for LLM inference measuring generated tokens per second — the single most common latency headline number.
full_explanation: TPS comes in two flavors: TPOT (time per output token — decode speed) and TTFT (time to first token — prefill latency). Different model+hardware combos can report TPS anywhere from 20 (huge model on CPU) to 1000+ (small quantized model on Groq LPUs). Typical production ranges: 50–200 TPS for Claude / GPT-4-class on current infrastructure, 200–500 TPS for 7B-class open models on A100/H100.
why_it_matters: TPS is the headline benchmarking number for inference. Know how to read it and what bottlenecks it exposes.

title: Time to First Token (TTFT)
short_definition: The latency between sending a request and receiving the first generated token — dominated by prefill time, critical for perceived responsiveness.
full_explanation: TTFT depends on prefill cost, which scales roughly linearly with prompt length. For a 500-token prompt on H100, TTFT might be 200–500ms; for a 50k-token prompt it can be several seconds. Optimizations: prompt caching (skip the prefix), prefix caching (engine-level KV reuse), chunked prefill, and smaller models. For user-facing chat, TTFT < 500ms feels responsive, > 2s feels laggy.
why_it_matters: TTFT is what users feel as 'speed.' Optimizing it is UX-critical for interactive LLM products.

title: Cost per Query (CPQ)
short_definition: The total LLM spend for a single user interaction — including input tokens, output tokens, reasoning tokens, tool calls, and embedding lookups.
full_explanation: CPQ captures the real unit economics. Components: prompt tokens (system + history + retrieved context + user input) × input price, output tokens × output price, reasoning tokens × reasoning price, embedding calls for retrieval, and tool-call overhead. For RAG chatbots, input dominates; for reasoning agents, reasoning and output dominate. Tracking CPQ by feature is table stakes for cost management.
why_it_matters: CPQ decides whether an LLM feature can ship profitably. It is the most important product-level metric in LLM ops.

title: Chain of Density
short_definition: A summarization prompting technique that iteratively adds key entities to a summary while keeping word count constant, producing dense, information-rich summaries.
full_explanation: Chain of Density (Adams et al. 2023) asks the model to: (1) write a general summary, (2) identify missing entities, (3) rewrite at the same length with the new entities included, (4) repeat. The summary densifies over iterations without growing in length. Produces more information per word than single-pass summarization and often matches human-written summaries on entity coverage and fluency. A rare-but-powerful technique for high-stakes summarization.
why_it_matters: Chain of Density produces better summaries than single-pass prompting, especially for news and research abstracts.

title: OpenAI o4 / Frontier Reasoning
short_definition: The post-o1 reasoning-model lineage (o3, o4-mini, o4, and beyond) representing the cutting edge of LLM reasoning via RL on verifiable tasks and extended test-time compute.
full_explanation: Frontier reasoning models (2025–2026) share several architectural moves: training on RL with correctness-verifier signals (for math and code), very long internal reasoning traces, tool use integrated into the reasoning loop, and high test-time compute budgets. Performance on ARC-AGI, competition math (AIME, IMO), code contests, and scientific reasoning has climbed sharply. Open alternatives: DeepSeek-R1, Qwen-QwQ, community RL fine-tunes of Llama.
why_it_matters: Frontier reasoning is the current research frontier. Most new benchmark records come from this model class.

title: Test-Time Compute
short_definition: The paradigm of spending more compute at inference time — via longer chains of thought, best-of-N, tree search — to improve performance on hard tasks without retraining.
full_explanation: Test-time compute (TTC) is a new scaling axis alongside parameters and training data. Techniques: longer chains of thought, self-consistency over many samples, tree-of-thought, best-of-N with reward model, process-reward-model-guided search. OpenAI's o1 paper demonstrated that TTC scaling produces reliable quality gains — often matching or exceeding what more parameters would provide. This reframes inference economics: you can buy quality by spending tokens.
why_it_matters: Test-time compute is the defining concept of the reasoning-model era. Understanding it is key to planning 2026+ product features.

title: Inference Time Scaling
short_definition: The empirical observation that LLM performance on reasoning tasks continues to improve predictably as you spend more tokens or compute at inference.
full_explanation: Inference-time scaling laws (Snell et al. 2024, OpenAI o1 report 2024) show that for many reasoning tasks, accuracy improves smoothly with log of inference-time compute — mirroring the training-compute scaling laws of Kaplan and Chinchilla. Techniques: majority voting over more samples, longer CoT, PRM-guided search, and repeated refinement. Practical implication: for hard tasks, pay at inference rather than retrain from scratch.
why_it_matters: Inference-time scaling is a third axis (alongside parameters and training data) to spend on quality. A major strategic lever.

title: Reasoning Model (Category)
short_definition: An LLM specifically trained (usually via RL) to produce extended internal reasoning before answering, excelling at math, coding, and scientific problems.
full_explanation: Reasoning models diverge from standard chat models in training and behavior. They are trained with reinforcement learning on tasks with verifiable answers (math, code), learning to produce long chains of thought that significantly outperform single-shot attempts. Examples: OpenAI o1/o3/o4, Claude extended thinking mode, DeepSeek-R1, Qwen-QwQ, Gemini 2 Thinking. Trade-offs: much higher cost and latency, sometimes worse general conversation quality.
why_it_matters: Reasoning models are the right tool for hard analytical tasks. Knowing when to reach for one matters for product design.

title: Open LLM Leaderboard
short_definition: Hugging Face's leaderboard that ranks open-weights LLMs on a standardized benchmark suite — the most-consulted reference for 'what open model should I try?'
full_explanation: The Open LLM Leaderboard runs submitted models through a fixed set of benchmarks (MMLU-Pro, GPQA, MATH, BBH, MuSR, IFEval in v2) and publishes results in a sortable table. Anyone can submit any model. V1 (2023) was dominated by benchmark-gamed fine-tunes; V2 (2024) uses harder, less-contaminated benchmarks. Complemented by LMSYS Chatbot Arena for preference-based ranking.
why_it_matters: The leaderboard is the default 'shopping' interface for open-weights LLMs. Read it with its known biases in mind.
